title: Football fans believe Inter Miami exposed Lionel Messi’s reason for crying after FIFA World Cup
description: After seeing Inter Miami’s blunder against the Chicago Fire, many fans are convinced that Lionel Messi isn’t going to be happy to return to the team. Messi is set to miss some time from Inter Miami as he recovers and rests following a gruelling FIFA World Cup campaign.
fullText:
After seeing Inter Miami’s blunder against the Chicago Fire, many fans are convinced that Lionel Messi isn’t going to be happy to return to the team.

Messi is set to miss some time from Inter Miami as he recovers and rests following a gruelling FIFA World Cup campaign.

But after seeing Rocco Novo, the Argentine goalkeeper for Inter Miami’s mistake against the Chicago Fire, he might want to extend his time away from the team.

During Inter Miami’s game against the Chicago Fire, Rocco Novo asked Facundo Mura to pass him the ball as the Fire players continued to press high up the field.

However, as he tried to clear the ball, Novo completely mistimed his kick, and it resulted in an own-goal that gave the Fire a 1-0 lead.

As soon as the clip of this incident emerged on X, it went viral as many fans remarked on the level of play in the MLS, less than a week after one of the best FIFA World Cup campaigns came to an end.

One fan wrote, “Messi wasn’t crying because he lost the World Cup, he was crying because he knew he had to go back to this.”

Another fan continued the joke, adding, “Is he just playing in MLS for s—- and giggles or what?”

A third fan used this as an opportunity to praise Messi, noting, “Dawg, Messi has been carrying s— players all his life!”

“You mean go back to this freedom? Weak league, everybody gets goals and assists, defenders keep making mistakes,” wrote a fourth fan who suggested Messi enjoys the seemingly low level of play in the MLS.

A fifth fan remarked, “Bro won the World Cup and 8 Ballon d’Ors just to play against Chicago Fire in 95°F heat on a Tuesday afternoon.”

And one final fan lamented Messi not playing in the top leagues in Europe, saying, “Such a shame, man, should’ve been in Europe this whole time.”

Coming into the tournament, few could have predicted Lionel Messi to have the kind of campaign that he did with Argentina.

Not only did he help lead Argentina back to the World Cup final, but he also did so while still being the best player on the team by a comfortable margin at 39 years of age.

Many people had presumed that Messi would be a diminished version of himself at this age and wouldn’t deliver the performances we saw from him in his prime.

But the magician from Rosario managed to bag eight goals and four assists in the tournament. And even though his performance in the final against Spain was unremarkable, it doesn’t take away from his stellar campaign.

Many fans believe that Messi reminded the world that, even at his age and after playing in the MLS, he is still one of the best players in the world and could easily thrive at the highest level.

Perhaps he finds an opportunity to return to FC Barcelona before he ends his career, as such a legendary chapter of his career ending on such a sour note won’t sit right with him in the long run.

title: Donald Trump set for further scrutiny as new FIFA complaint to be lodged after the World Cup
description: The World Cup came to an end at the weekend, but the controversy surrounding the President of the United States and Folarin Balogun’s suspension U-turn is not going away any time soon. Mauricio Pochettino’s men have not kicked a ball in weeks having been knocked out of the World Cup in the last 16 by Belgium.
fullText:
The World Cup came to an end at the weekend, but the controversy surrounding the President of the United States and Folarin Balogun’s suspension U-turn is not going away any time soon.

Mauricio Pochettino’s men have not kicked a ball in weeks having been knocked out of the World Cup in the last 16 by Belgium.

However, their legacy from this summer’s competition has been tarnished following what was a promising summer for the USMNT.

The saga surrounding Balogun’s sudden availability to play in that game against Belgium has not gone down well in the world of soccer.

Trump was involved in the process that led FIFA to make a U-turn on the striker’s red card suspension, causing many people to question the morality of the decision.

Although the USMNT did not benefit from the debacle, there are nations like Norway that do not what to see anything like this ever happen again.

Trump admitted he called Gianni Infantino regarding Balogun’s red card, with the president suggesting the suspension should be overturned.

It was a completely unprecedented situation that took the world of soccer by shock, but FIFA are now being urged to admit the decision was wrong.

A report from The Times has claimed that Lise Klaveness, the president of the Norwegian Football Federation, wants an answer from Infantino.

The Norwegian soccer governing body has admitted they do not want to see this controversy simply swept “under the carpet”.

Although the saga likely hindered the USMNT heading into the game in Seattle, it is something that soccer fans do not want to see anything like this ever happen again.

There are serious concerns that political neutrality was breached given the close relationship Trump and Infantino have seemingly formed.

In recent days, it has been suggested that Trump has suggested Infantino is given a role in the United Nations.

Clearly there is a feeling that the relationship between Trump and the FIFA president compromised the ethics of the World Cup.

What will come of Norway’s protest will certainly be interesting.

title: Little margin for error in Newcastle's bid to be a top club by 2030
description: As the fifth anniversary of Newcastle United's Saudi-led takeover approaches in October, the club clearly has work to do on and off the field.  Chairman Yasir Al-Rumayyan set out to be "number one", while chief executive David Hopkinson's aim was to be "in the debate about being the top club in the world" by 2030 - but Newcastle need the infrastructure to match.  An announcement is awaited on plans for a new state-of-the-art training ground after a site was identified in Woolsington near Newcastle Airport.
fullText:
As the fifth anniversary of Newcastle United's Saudi-led takeover approaches in October, the club clearly has work to do on and off the field.

Chairman Yasir Al-Rumayyan set out to be "number one", while chief executive David Hopkinson's aim was to be "in the debate about being the top club in the world" by 2030 - but Newcastle need the infrastructure to match.

An announcement is awaited on plans for a new state-of-the-art training ground after a site was identified in Woolsington near Newcastle Airport.

The club have stressed the importance of taking time to make the right decision when it comes to either expanding St James' Park or building a new stadium, which will be key to boosting revenue.

To consistently compete, however, there is little margin for error.

Newcastle needed to see more of an immediate return from their £100m-plus net spend last summer, for example, but defender Malick Thiaw has been the only unquestioned success.

The club are also counting the cost of a bruising 12th-placed finish last season after missing out on Europe.

Most pertinently, after breaching Uefa's financial sustainability regulations, Newcastle have entered into a three-year settlement with European football's governing body.

Raising funds through the sales of Anthony Gordon and Sandro Tonali to Barcelona and Tottenham respectively was therefore crucial in order for the club to have the ability to reinvest this summer.

Three key players have now left in less than 12 months, though, which has weakened the side considerably, following Alexander Isak's painful £125m departure to Liverpool last summer.

Newcastle do not want to lose another big name in Bruno Guimaraes, but the captain's future is uncertain after he intimated he wished to join Arsenal.

Whatever happens, trading will remain a key part of the club's strategy in the coming years - and there has been a shift in approach with incomings too.

Further reinforcements are sorely needed but all of the club's arrivals this summer - goalkeeper Ewen Jaouen, midfielder Sean Steur and winger Bazoumana Toure - have been aged 20 and under, and from the continent.

The trio have immense potential and in the right hands could flourish, but they will need time.

It feels like the same could be said for the project as a whole right now.

title: Arsenal consider two centre-backs as cover for William Saliba after major injury blow
description: The Gunners are willing to spend big on a centre-back as cover for Saliba, who is expected to miss significant time despite not needing surgery to fix a back problem sustained during the World Cup with France
fullText:
Arsenal are looking into a significant investment at centre-back with William Saliba expected to be out long-term following a major injury blow.

The France centre-back has returned from the World Cup with a back injury and while surgery is not required, he is expected to be out for an extended period.

The Independent understands that the Gunners will therefore pursue a new centre-back in the coming weeks as cover and are prepared to spend big to add depth to the position, rather than bring in a player on loan.

Although there is cover for Saliba in the shape of Piero Hincapie, Riccardo Califiori, Jurrien Timber, Cristhian Mosquera and Ben White, some of that group’s own injury issues and Mikel Arteta’s previous preference to avoid shifting his first-choice full-backs has led to a decision to spend again at centre-back this summer.

The Independent understands Aston Villa’s Ezri Konsa and John Stones, available on a free transfer after his Man City contract expired, are both being considered.

The new signing will immediately have the opportunity to partner Gabriel Magalhaes as the champions begin their Premier League title defence.

An Arsenal statement on Wednesday confirmed: “Subsequent specialist reviews on William’s return to London this week have confirmed that he has suffered a back injury which will require a period of rehabilitation. Extensive assessments have concluded that surgery is not recommended, but William should now commence a managed recovery programme.

“William’s rehabilitation will commence immediately, with continual management of his injury, and he is expected to be out of action for an extended period. Everyone is fully focused on supporting William to ensure he is back to full fitness as soon as possible.”

Arsenal could also receive significant compensation from Fifa due to Saliba’s injury occurring during the World Cup, with Fifa stating that “players can receive up to $7.5m (£5.6m) per accident, paid daily for a maximum of 365 days after the first 28 days of absence. Payments are calculated on the player's fixed salary only, excluding bonuses or other variable pay.”

Arsenal are expected to spend in other departments too, having missed out on Morgan Rogers after walking away from talks with Aston Villa once the price reached £117m, with Chelsea swooping in to sign the England star.

In addition to a new centre-back and a starting winger, Arsenal could add further depth in the wide areas given Leandro Trossard’s move to Besiktas.

The Gunners play Man City in the Community Shield on 16 August in Cardiff before entertaining Coventry on 21 August to kick off their league campaign.

title: Fans accuse Donald Trump of lying about his height after World Cup moment with Spain’s Rodri
description: World Cup fans are making fun of US President Donald Trump. The 2026 FIFA World Cup concluded with Spain being crowned as the champions of the world for the second time in the tournament’s history.
fullText:
World Cup fans are making fun of US President Donald Trump.

The 2026 FIFA World Cup concluded with Spain being crowned as the champions of the world for the second time in the tournament’s history.

Since the final was held in the US, it was President Donald Trump who handed the trophy and medals to Spain after they registered a 1-0 win over Argentina.

Spain’s Rodri won the 2026 World Cup Golden Ball, and the trophy was handed to him by Trump. At the time, fans didn’t think much of it, but the clip has now gone viral on X.

Why? Well, fans believe that it exposed Trump lying about his height as he was dwarfed in comparison to Rodri. After all, both Trump and Rodri are reportedly 6’3″, but it didn’t seem like it on stage.

“Trump in heels and at least 4-5 inches shorter,” a fan wrote. While another added, “The orange s___ is a good 6″ shorter than the 6’3″ Rodri.”

“Proving Trump is nowhere near 6’3″ because Rodri is,” a third fan pointed out. “One of these people is 6’3″ tall… and the other is President Trump,” a fourth fan joked.

“He’s definitely not 6’3″! Look at the height difference between him and Rodri, who is actually verified to be 6’3″! Trump might be only 5’9″!” a fifth fan announced.

While it may have been due to the camera angle, most fans are now convinced that Trump has lied about his height. Either that, or he has shrunk due to ageing as the US President is already 80 years old.

title: Xteink X3 is the little e-reader the world's not quite ready for
description: The market for small e-readers has been booming lately. Earlier this year, Jason took a look at the Xteink X4, a pint-sized reading device that was about as light and small as they come. But those…
fullText:
The market for small e-readers has been booming lately. Earlier this year, Jason took a look at the Xteink X4, a pint-sized reading device that was about as light and small as they come. But those maniacs at Xteink weren’t done yet: they followed it up with the $79 X3, which is unbelievably even lighter and smaller.

I wouldn’t blame you for wondering if there’s such a thing as too small and light—at 3.84 inches by 2.51 inches (and just 0.2 inches thick) the X3 is still larger than, say, a credit card, but not by much. The screen itself measures just 3.7 inches, more than half an inch smaller than the X4’s 4.3 inch, but its 259 ppi resolution still makes it plenty easy to read on.1 At that size it’s little imposition to take it with you wherever you go. Easy to lose? Maybe. Easy to love? Yes.

For one thing, this small size unlocks at least one capability that the X4 couldn’t fully deliver upon—the ability to stick to the back of your phone. Like the X4, the X3 has a MagSafe-compatible magnet in its rear case and it fits just about perfectly on the back of my iPhone 17 Pro, nestled below the camera plateau.

However, the magnet is not particularly strong, and it lacks the secondary stabilizing magnet that you see on official MagSafe accessories, meaning it wobbles around a bit. I wouldn’t call it a super secure connection, but it’s pretty good if you’re just slipping it in and out of a pocket. Just be aware that it probably will come loose.

Like the X4 before it, the X3 features a pair of rocker switches on the front for a total of four possible buttons. But they’ve opted to skip the X4’s side mounted rocker switch in favor of a single buttons on each side. There’s also an on/off switch at the top (plus a recessed button used for resetting the device). And with all of that, they somehow managed to continue to fit a microSD slot in the top. The bottom left corner also has a little set of holes for a lanyard or wrist strap.

The one casualty of the smaller X3, however, is the port. The X4 was big enough to accommodate a USB-C connector, but the X3’s thin profile makes that impossible. Instead, there’s a pogo-pin USB cable that connects to four magnetic contacts on the reader. Is this a step back? Strictly speaking, yes. Using a proprietary connector is a bummer in the year 2026, especially because it means having to keep track of a single-use cable. That said, once you get the X3 up and running, the only real need for the connector is charging, and the battery on this thing lasts a very long time. (I’ve yet to run it down in several weeks of usage.) Just remember to make a note of where you stow that cable for the point months down the road when you do need to charge it and find yourself wondering whatever happened to it.

As with the X4, the X3 lacks any sort of screen lighting, meaning that it’s strictly a “read in bright light” device. But don’t worry, because Xteink has already unveiled a new X4 Pro with the company’s first lit screen.2

All in all, I found the X3 very comfortable to read on, whether attached to my iPhone or not. The side buttons work great as page-turners, and the screen is high-contrast and sharp enough to make text render very clearly. All in all, it’s a great reading experience—if you take into account one major caveat.

The elephant in the room for the Xteink X3 is the software. Xteink ships the X3 with its own built-in firmware which, by all accounts, has improved since the first version. But I can’t really tell you, because upon opening the box and powering up the device, the first thing I did was install Crosspoint, the open-source community-built firmware that has turned this device from a curiosity into a real contender.3

Unlike a lot of other firmware replacements, Crosspoint is super easy to install; you can do it right from a web browser. While it’s not fully free of technological hurdles, I’d say that most people comfortable using an e-reader or updating their iPhones will be able to handle the process with few problems. And the benefits are manifold.

Crosspoint goes beyond simply turning this into a functional device and into one that is actually fun to use. It’s got a straightforward, easy to navigate interface that makes it simple to do everything from reading to loading books (accomplished via a web-based interface). For those who like to tweak their reading setup, there are plenty of configuration options, everything from screen orientation to hyphenation and, yes, custom fonts loaded via the SD card.

Even the onscreen keyboard that you need to use for various configuration tasks is pretty solid for what’s available—on par with navigating via an onscreen keyboard with a game controller, in my opinion. I wouldn’t want to have to type in my Wi-Fi password every time—and you don’t, because it saves it for you—but one time was fine.

There are also a bunch of other features that might appeal to those who want to take reading to the next level. For example, support for Open Publication Distribution System (OPDS) standard means that if you want to host your book library on your own server, you can point your reader at that and download any of the books you already own directly to your device. There’s also support for KOreader sync to help keep your place between supported devices.4

Crosspoint has been so successful, in fact, that the team at Xteink has smartened up and started collaborating. (This after a brief moment when it looked like the company might try to firmware lock its devices.) Now, not only do the “developer editions” sold directly by the company continue to allow you to install the custom firmware, the company has been working with the Crosspoint team to make sure new devices, like the X4 Pro, will already be supported when they arrive. While Xteink hasn’t quite adopted Crosspoint as the default OS, it’s not far off.

Given how positive I’ve been above, you might be all too tempted to run out and snap up an Xteink X3 for yourself. I don’t blame you! At $79 (and often discounted for less than that), it’s well positioned as an impulse buy. And while I don’t want to dissuade you, I have to note the biggest caveat of all: the world.

If there’s one thing that’s holding back the X3 and the X4 and, frankly, all the other wacky readers out there, it’s the market. The e-reader space is still unquestionably dominated by Amazon purveying its razors-and-blades strategy: buy the Kindle for cheap5 and the company earns it all back when you buy your books or Kobo, who makes the hardware that I prefer, is a distant second, and has some advantages, but operates on roughly a similar scheme.

Both of these companies benefit from lock-in in the form of Digital Rights Management. DRM is, of course, largely pushed by publishers, who insist upon cryptographic schemes to lock down the books they sell in order to nominally protect against piracy. Never mind the fact that books are still easily and widely pirated, the DRM is relatively simple to crack6, and many of those books have been vacuumed up by the boatload by an even more insidious thief in the form of AI training data.

I come at this, of course, from the perspective of both reader and writer. As a writer, I certainly want to be compensated for all the hard work I put in to create my books, but I also want people to read and enjoy them. That’s why you’ll find no stauncher defender of libraries.

Personally, I find the industry’s insistence on DRM misplaced, just as I did with the music industry two decades ago. The lack of these security features does not automatically turn people at large into thieves and it’s insulting to think that it would. In fact, the sci-fi publisher Tor, one of the biggest names in the business and a subsidiary of Big Five publisher Macmillan, has put out its titles without DRM for almost fifteen years and I haven’t seen any significant indication that it’s harmed their business. Many of my books are also available directly from my publisher without DRM, and I don’t think that’s put a dent in my bottom line either.

I would love to be able to easily load any book I’ve ever purchased onto my Xteink devices—or, for that matter, any e-reader or e-reading app of my choice. There are, of course, extensive repositories of DRM-free public domain books available. Amazon, to its credit, does now allow you to download DRM-free copies of any book that you’ve purchased.7 I argued a few years back that this is an opportunity for Apple to distinguish its stagnant ebook marketplace from the competition. But for first-run books, your options are currently more limited (and sometimes harder to suss out).

I’d also love to somehow see support for Libby, the popular library ebook borrowing app, on Xteink devices, though I don’t see that happening anytime soon. But in my opinion, the bottom line is that more ways to read makes more readers of books, which in turn makes more customers of books.

The other week I was riding the train with my kid, our usual weekend pastime. As he was enjoying looking out the window, I was reading a book on the X3, magneted to the back of my iPhone 17 Pro. As we neared a stop, a woman nearby caught sight of it and marveled over it. “Is that a tiny e-reader?”

I extolled its virtues for a moment before they got off at their stop. But it told me one important thing: there’s a hunger for these types of devices. Because people who self-select as readers in this day and age are, above all, passionate. You have to make a choice to be sitting and reading a book when you could be watching TikTok or Instagram or listening to music or a podcast, all of which are constantly at your fingertips.

That’s where the X3 (and the X4) deliver. A Kindle or Kobo isn’t exactly bulky, but they’re not pocketable either. Being able to take your e-reader with you just as we stuffed mass market paperbacks in our back pockets in days gone by is freeing and fun. Now if the world might just shift a little bit more to meet us, maybe we could free ourselves a little further.

[Dan Moren is the East Coast Bureau Chief of Six Colors, as well as an author, podcaster, and two-time Jeopardy! champion. You can find him on Mastodon at @dmoren@zeppelin.flights or reach him by email at dan@sixcolors.com. His next novel, the sci-fi adventure Eternity's Tomb, will be released in November 2026.]

title: BrainStem: Open‑source bio‑inspired AI with 12 neuromodulators for learning
description: A neuro-symbolic cognitive architecture that transforms text corpora into a probabilistic relational world model using an SQLite backend. A homeostatic loop of 12 digital neurotransmitters autonomo...
fullText:
BrainStem is a biologically inspired, neuro-symbolic cognitive architecture for lifelong learning. It is designed to learn models of the structures and dynamics of language and text through context hypotheses, uncertainty, contradiction, revision, neuromodulation, replay, and consolidation rather than by merely storing isolated facts.

YouTube - BrainStem Project AI conversation 22.07.26

NotebookLM codebase exploration 22.07.26

BrainStem is a research and calibration system, not a production-ready assistant. Permanent fact, relation, and question writes remain locked while the learning core and its candidate flow are being validated.

BrainStem has completed the original Stage A foundation work and the Legacy Cleanup large slices A–C. Historical and unused learning paths were removed, including the obsolete nlp.py path, the legacy CorpusReader / Phase-3d word-role and filter path, and the historical Phase-4def–4p cluster. The current runtime uses the canonical Phase-7cort top path.

Additional completed foundation work includes:

The GUI chunk counter is the canonical corpus-read progress indicator. Counts of distinct context_hypotheses.chunk_id values are treated only as hypothesis-coverage proxies and not as an exact processed-chunk counter.

The Modern Gap Candidate Bridge and non-productive Phase-5f Shadow Observation path remain installed in the untouched canonical runtime.

Both Candidate and Observation frontiers reached the full current hypothesis population. Initial Shadow Observation generation completed without projection errors. The original V1 observation identity mixed stable identity with source_updated_at, causing repeated observations of the same hypothesis to appear as new keys instead of versions of one stable identity.

Stable Observation Identity V2 is installed as a parallel, non-productive dual-write path.

The first production parity cycle proved exact V1/V2 input parity. During the subsequent natural long run, V2 recorded:

A dedicated driver audit proved that all 136,990 recorded source-state changes were caused only by source_updated_at. The stored source content and the projection fingerprint remained unchanged. This established that the timestamp-sensitive V2 source fingerprint was technically consistent but too sensitive for semantic versioning.

A parallel Content-Stable Fingerprint Shadow Classifier is installed without changing the existing V2 writer.

The Classifier currently has no production events because the normal Observation checkpoint is fully caught up and no new context_hypotheses or Candidate Shadow source row exists beyond it. Fifty additional system cycles were verified as zero-input cycles for this path.

Post-corpus replay activity continues after all corpus chunks have been read. Phase 6a creates replay candidates and replay events, but its existing context_hypotheses update writes only replay-control and replay-provenance fields. It does not update semantic hypothesis fields and does not update the canonical context_hypotheses.updated_at field.

A future-only, context_hypotheses-scoped Replay-Control Shadow Capture is installed.

After correction of the installer hook to use the actual SQLite connection receiver db, the natural production test recorded exactly:

Each of the 60 observed hypotheses received five replay events, with one initial state and four stable refreshes. This proves natural runtime reachability, exact event parity, retry-safe identity and stable replay-control classification. It does not prove semantic learning.

Phase 6a currently computes outcome_observation_available from the global row count of phase5g_experiment_outcomes. It does not join by replay event, hypothesis identity or post-replay observation time. The table is currently empty, so all recorded Phase-6a cycles report no available outcome observation.

The current legacy Phase-5g experiment producer exists in v8_phase5g_context_strategy_selection_and_experiment_memory_release.py, but it relies on internal_learning_gaps as its primary source. internal_learning_gaps remains intentionally empty and productively closed.

The producer is not statically connected to the current Registry or canonical Phase-7cort cycle. If activated, it would write operationally to Phase-5g experiments, reading queue, attention and gap-related structures. It is therefore not a safe read-only bridge for the modern Shadow architecture.

The Phase-5h outcome writer exists and can evaluate Phase-5g strategy experiments, but:

The current Phase-5g and Phase-5h path is an internal strategy-projection and evaluation path, not a proven independent real-outcome path.

A read-only preflight proved that V2 Stable Observations contain all fields required for a future-only, non-productive Phase-5g Candidate contract.

No installer has been authorized. The live V2 projection population currently shows almost no strategy diversity:

The next pending read-only audit is the Modern V2 Projection Input Variance, Formula Drivers & Strategy Diversity Audit. It has been prepared but not yet executed.

During long autonomous runs, the GUI became unresponsive while the autonomous worker continued progressing. The worker was later stopped with taskkill, and SQLite quick_check and integrity_check both remained successful.

The GUI became responsive again after restarting with the same large database. Therefore:

Traditional semantic systems often focus on the what: storing and retrieving content. BrainStem focuses on the how: learning how context, uncertainty, evidence, contradiction, revision, and consolidation interact over time.

A corpus is treated as training substrate rather than as a static knowledge base. The active learning architecture forms and revises context hypotheses, preserves errors as learning material, and delays permanent knowledge promotion until consolidation and safety gates are validated.

BrainStem is an autonomous software architecture designed for continuous, self-improving data processing and knowledge management. At its core, the system operates through an Autonomous Loop that orchestrates a chain of learning phases to ingest, analyze, and refine information without manual intervention.

It is important to understand that the biological terminology used throughout the project’s technical documentation, such as "neuromodulators," "sleep," or "homeostasis" serves as a set of metaphors to describe the underlying digital functions. These biological terms are mapped to mathematical variables and algorithmic states that steer the system's behavior.

Dynamic Steering Variables: What the documentation calls "digital messenger substances" are actually dynamic meta-parameters. These variables, such as "dopamine" or "serotonin" equivalents, represent numerical values that adjust the system's learning rate, error weighting, and exploration strategies in real-time.

Active vs. Offline Processing: The system cycles between an active ingestion phase and an optimization phase. During active processing, the system extracts "context hypotheses" from new data inputs. During the optimization phase (metaphorically called "sleep"), the system re-evaluates these recorded hypotheses through batch replay and consolidation to improve overall accuracy and stability.

Knowledge Distillation: By comparing new data against existing stable records, the system filters out inconsistencies and promotes reliable information into its long-term memory structures.

Equilibrium Control: To prevent the control variables from reaching unproductive extreme values (saturation), the system uses stability monitoring routines. These routines act as a feedback mechanism that pulls meta-parameters back into a functional range when the system detects a performance plateau or excessive variance.

Adaptive Boundaries: The limits within which the system operates are not hardcoded but self-regulating. The software learns from its own performance metrics (L2M metrics) to expand or contract its processing thresholds based on the complexity of the data it encounters.

In summary, the project is a recursive learning engine that uses bio-inspired metaphors to implement a highly flexible, self-governing control logic for automated knowledge acquisition.

Runtime phases are loaded through ki_system/phase_registry.py. The registry defines load order, isolates module-loading failures, and verifies the managed-cycle top phase.

7cort Cortisol → 7g BDNF → 7f Orexin → 7e Histamine → 7d Slow-Wave → 7c E/I → 7b1 Wake-Chain Bridge → 7b Endocannabinoids → 7a Adenosine → 6d → 6c → 6b → 6a

The cleaned chain uses Phase 7b1 as orchestrator so a normal global cycle produces one complete Phase-6a replay path, one Phase-7c E/I event, one Phase-7d cycle, and one workpoint-observer event.

BrainStem currently uses 12 digital neuromodulators. Their values are normalized to [0.0, 1.0] and derived from internal system state under bounded, biologically inspired dynamics and homeostatic constraints.

GABA currently regulates system-level inhibition. It does not identify or suppress individual words, relations, or extraction errors.

All 12 displays are connected both statically and at runtime in the GUI.

Phase 6a performs offline-style replay after the wake path. Phase 6b evaluates replay effectiveness and plasticity adjustments. The critic gate checks whether proposed changes remain consistent enough to be retained; rejected or unstable material remains available as error and revision evidence.

Phase 7d adds sub-1-Hz up/down-state processing with:

A passive Phase-7d workpoint observer records longitudinal E/I state, activity, survivor ratios, reference movement, and virtual adjustment proposals without applying them.

This separation prevents Phase 6a from overwriting the active Phase-7c state on the next cycle.

Stage B will not open all write capabilities at once. The controlled sequence is:

Stage B remains blocked until the current Shadow bridge has demonstrably processed real candidates.

The following productive paths remain disabled:

The active architecture does not use word blacklists or hard-coded linguistic filters.

BrainStem uses ki_memory.sqlite3 in the project root. The database is created automatically when absent.

Learning state can be reset without re-importing the corpus. Preserved content includes documents, chunks, FTS data, import state, and configuration. The reset workflow performs a dry run and creates a timestamped database backup before applying changes.

Performance indexes are ensured during bootstrap. Bounded pruning is limited to explicitly approved history tables. Active state, Phase-5f/5g/5i data, and other protected tables are excluded from generic pruning.

Periodic autonomous execution of the approved pruning routine remains an open maintenance item.

The GUI includes a sensory-deprivation mode that skips new wake/read input while replay, consolidation, and neuromodulatory dynamics continue.

The completed 1,344-cycle no-input test supported the Stage-A stability decision.

The GUI remains an experimental testing interface; individual areas may still be incomplete.

A Windows zimdump.exe build and its required DLL files must be placed in the project root next to main.py. Users must provide their own ZIM corpus.

The current development corpus is the German Wikipedia category Computer, imported into roughly 102,000 chunks.

After major refactorings, project documentation includes a Legacy Report with:

BrainStem does not claim that every current hypothesis is meaningful or that the system understands language at a human level. The current objective is to establish and validate the mechanisms by which hypotheses are formed, challenged, revised, inhibited, replayed, and consolidated.

Current zero-result Shadow measurements do not prove a defect and do not prove successful candidate processing. Candidate-flow evidence is the next required result.

BrainStem is an experimental cognitive-architecture research project. Biological terminology is used as an engineering analogy and design inspiration. The software is not a biological simulation and does not claim neuroscientific equivalence.

title: The borough where families are slowly disappearing
description: Islington is a snapshot of a trend playing out across inner London, Britain and much of the world.
fullText:
Islington seems, on the surface, a family-friendly area. Parents push buggies through quiet leafy side streets of this popular neighbourhood that is also within easy reach of central London.

But this north London borough has one of the lowest fertility rates in the country and mothers are older on average here than in most other parts of the capital.

Islington is a snapshot of a trend also playing out not only in inner London but also other parts of the world.

The situation has been described by the London Assembly as an "existential challenge" for the city as a whole, so what are the reasons behind this and what can be done about it?

Keen to find out more about family life in Islington, I joined a Zip-Zap baby music class to speak to parents.

Tiphanie Frutuoso and her husband had their first baby this year.

"My husband and I have a one-bedroom flat – a two-bedroom is super expensive.

"Childcare is £2,000-£3,000 a month."

They are moving back to Luxembourg, partly because childcare is free.

"It's really sad because we love London. We have better job opportunities here," Frutuoso added.

Tina Meschino had her first child at 41.

"I've always been career-driven. I moved to London from Australia for career opportunities.

"It's taken a while to get to where I want to be – an executive position – which felt like the right time to start a family."

But she added that it was very difficult to find a suitable home with enough space, within budget.

Samantha Nagaratnam had her daughter at 35.

"I was moving forward in my career and was really, really focused on that. But nobody tells you how hard it is [to fall pregnant]. It took about three years when we started trying."

Originally from Toronto, she said it was much harder to find a family home in Islington with enough space.

Zip-Zap co-founder Laura Edwards says families are moving out of Islington to other boroughs for better schools, more affordable homes and more space.

"It's going to have huge implications in 40-50 years when there's nobody to do the jobs and support the economy."

Some families have told me they have decided to stay in the borough but it is proving to be really difficult.

Father-of-two Patrick Penny-Annang has lived in Islington all his life and now shares a one-bedroom flat with his family.

"Our living room becomes our bedroom... our one bed becomes the kids' room," he said.

"Islington is a lovely area to live in but because of that it's really expensive.

"Schools have been closing, schools that I used to go to."

Between 2013 and 2023, London's population of 0–9-year-olds fell by 99,100, despite the capital's overall population growing by 506,000. The decline has been steeper in inner than outer London.

"It's an existential challenge for London," says Bassam Mahfouz, chairman of the London Assembly's Economy, Culture and Skills Committee.

His committee's recent report recommends pursuing UNICEF Child Friendly City status, introducing child-friendly housing standards, and mapping play and recreation spaces across boroughs.

"We need to tackle this as an absolute emergency.

"What's happening in Islington is happening right across London," he said, warning the trend threatens the city's workforce.

He is urging the government and the mayor to "redouble their efforts" to ensure "children are growing up in every postcode".

The fertility rate measures the number of children born per woman of childbearing age.

At 0.99, Islington has the second-lowest total fertility rate in the capital after the City of London, and one of the lowest in England.

To put it in perspective, the estimated number of children born per woman fell to just under 1.4 for England and Wales in 2025, down from 1.9 in 2010.

BBC England's data unit analysed some of the factors that may be contributing to Islington's low rate:

Young women leaving: In 2025, Islington recorded a net loss of 1,062 women aged 30-34, one of the biggest declines for that age group in the country

High rents: In 2023-24, renters spent 46% of the median local income on rent, giving Islington the ninth-highest rent-to-income ratio among 316 councils in England and Wales and only eight boroughs – all in London – ranked higher

Unaffordable housing: In 2025, the median house price was almost 12 times the median annual income, placing Islington among the 40 least affordable local authorities in England

Social housing demand: As of 31 March 2025, 16,794 households were on Islington's social housing waiting list – the 17th highest in England and sixth highest in London

Expensive childcare: Parents paid an average £9.39 an hour for two-year-olds - the 12th highest in England.

Francesca Baker-Brooker, 39, who works in PR, and her husband Andy, 41, an accountant, have decided not to have children.

The couple live in a two-bedroom flat in Islington. They both say they shared a "gut instinct" early in their relationship that parenthood was not for them.

"We like our lifestyle - we like running, going to festivals and gigs," Francesca said.

Soon after that conversation, they moved from a three-bedroom house in Kent to a two-bedroom flat in Islington and Andy had a vasectomy.

"I don't know how feasible it would have been to move to London if we'd wanted to start a family," Andy said.

When people ask if he has a family, Andy says his answer is: "I have a family – my wife."

They say about half of their friends in London do not have children, although they believe that is far less common outside the capital.

"I think it definitely is easier to be child-free in London," he added.

Katie Beck, a policy fellow at the London School of Economics' LSE Cities, said: "People want to have children in London, but there are barriers – namely the cost of living and childcare.

"People are not able to stay in London to have children, or once they do they're moving to outer boroughs or leaving London altogether."

Beck says London's falling rates of children reflects a wider global trend, driven by housing and childcare costs, more women entering higher education and the workforce, changing parenting expectations, fewer teenage pregnancies, and growing concerns about the future.

She points to boroughs such as Islington, where high poverty, limited green space, expensive housing and dense populations contribute to particularly low child populations.

I was keen to find out what the younger generation think, so spoke to a group of students.

Jess Rolf, 23, says having children was "never something she worried about", and trying to build a settled life was "very stressful with the cost of living and the job market".

"I'm from Lincolnshire and people my age back home are buying houses with their partners and talking about starting families.

"I don't think that's on the cards for me."

Charlotte Ward, 26, has said planning ahead is difficult.

"A lot of people our age are quite disenfranchised.

"You put all your energy into school, university and work, then wonder, 'do I want to bring somebody else into that if I can't even support myself right now?'

"When parents or grandparents ask 'what happens when you have children?', I can say, 'I might not'.

"There's a small element of emancipation from the expectation that you definitely will have children but if I'm progressing in my career and my male counterparts don't have to take time out to have a child, that could put me at a disadvantage."

Millicent Angel, 24, says it is hard to envisage a future when the world feels "so unstable" but she still definitely wants children.

She adds there's "a million and one things" she would like to do first, including developing her career.

Beck argues that "children are an indicator species".

"They are a sign of a healthy city. If you see a dearth of children, that rings alarm bells that a city is not functioning as it should," she added.

She describes a child-friendly city as one that is accessible, with active transport and safe affordable housing.

As the independent think tank Centre for London points out: "London is not becoming a city without families with children, but it is becoming a place where the realities that families with children face are increasingly unequal."

A spokesperson for the Mayor of London, Sir Sadiq Khan said: "The mayor wants London to remain a city for everyone – where people can afford to put down roots, raise a family and build a future.

"That is why tackling London's housing crisis and increasing the supply of genuinely affordable homes is one of his top priorities, alongside measures to support families with the cost of living, including funding free school meals for every child in a state primary school."

They added that he would "continue working with the government to build more affordable homes, support public services and ensure London remains a great place for families and children".

A government spokesperson said: "We've made paternity and unpaid parental leave day one rights through our Employment Rights Act, and have rolled out 30 hours of funded childcare a week to working families."

The government added that it was also building more council homes to back "families at every stage".

title: Global Hunger Falls From 35% in 1970 to 7.8%
description: Armed conflict and violence remain the primary drivers of acute food insecurity.
fullText:
Global hunger declined for the third consecutive year, according to the Food and Agriculture Organization's (FAO) The State of Food Security and Nutrition in the World 2026 report.

The new report estimates that 7.8 percent of the world's people were undernourished in 2025.

The FAO report notes that less than 2.5 percent of people living in the U.S., Canada, Eastern Asia, and Europe are undernourished. Therefore, its calculations chiefly encompass the extent of hunger in developing countries in Africa, Latin America, and the rest of Asia.

Armed conflict and violence remain the primary drivers of acute food insecurity in 12 out of 13 hunger hotspots identified by the World Food Programme and the FAO.

The drop to 7.8 percent is a huge improvement since 1970, when undernourishment afflicted nearly 35 percent of people living in developing countries.

The new estimate is down from 8.1 percent in 2024 and 8.5 percent in 2023. Overall, 645 million people were still facing hunger in 2025.

While that is good news, the new estimate remains above the pre-pandemic nadir of 7.2 percent in 2017. The FAO report projects that the level of hunger will decline by 20 percent during the rest of this decade, but that 510 million people will still be undernourished in 2030. This trend falls well short of the United Nations' sustainable development goal of zero hunger by 2030.

The post Global Hunger Falls From 35% in 1970 to 7.8% appeared first on Reason.com.

title: Glasgow set to welcome the world for scaled back Commonwealth Games
description: Athletes from 74 countries and territories will compete for 215 gold medals over 10 days of competition.
fullText:
When the fastest man on the planet won Commonwealth relay gold for Jamaica in 2014, more than 50,000 fans cheered him home at Hampden.

But the athletes seeking to emulate Usain Bolt when the Games return to Glasgow - including mile world record holder Josh Kerr - will be competing at Scotstoun Stadium in front of 11,000 spectators.

This time round almost everything is on a smaller scale but organisers are confident fans will create a Tartan Army-style atmosphere for the 3,000 competitors who will be chasing glory.

From Friday until 2 August, 215 gold medals will be up for grabs - compared to 261 when Scotland last staged the Games - but Glasgow 2026 will see a record 47 para-sports events.

The action gets under way with bowls and para-bowls heats at 09:30, but the Games will be officially opened by King Charles during a ceremony at the Hydro later.

It will be attended by athletes from 74 nations and territories as well as dignitaries including Prime Minister Andy Burnham, Scotland's First Minister John Swinney and the First Minister of Wales Rhun ap Iorwerth.

Some of the 4,000 Games volunteers, aged 16 to 96, will also take part in the celebration.

Glasgow stepped in when the Australian state of Victoria pulled out of hosting the Games in July 2023.

It had only agreed to stage the event in April 2022 after the Commonwealth Games Federation (CGF) struggled to find a host.

Organisers had originally estimated the event would cost A$2.6bn (£1.35bn) and the state government had billed it as a boost for the regions.

But the projected bill soared to more than A$6 billion (£3.11bn).

Victorian Premier Daniel Andrews said the new figure was "more than twice the estimated economic benefit" it would bring to the state.

The decision not only plunged the future of the 2026 edition into doubt but the Games as whole.

But in September 2024 a deal, backed by the Scottish government, was agreed for Glasgow to host "a scaled back, but high-quality" event.

It will be staged across four venues along an eight-mile stretch - Scotstoun Stadium, Tollcross International Swimming Centre, Glasgow International Arena and the Scottish Event Campus (SEC).

In 2014 the budget for the Games was £543m but no public money has been spent on the 23rd edition, which will cost £150m in total.

The CGF is investing £100m of this, with the remainder coming from commercial revenue streams such as ticketing, sponsorship, broadcast rights and merchandise.

Athletics, cycling and swimming have proved the most popular but tickets are still available for many events.

The triumphant 2012 London Olympics were still fresh in the mind when Glasgow last staged the Commonwealth Games.

Usain Bolt topped an athletics cast list which also included Olympic champions David Rudisha of Kenya (800m), Australia's Sally Pearson (100m hurdles) and Valerie Adams of New Zealand (shot put).

This year the most decorated track star is also a Jamaican sprinter.

Elaine Thompson-Herah made her Commonwealth debut in 2014 and went on to become the first woman in history to win back-to-back Olympic 100m and 200m titles in Rio and Tokyo.

She is aiming to defend the titles she won at Birmingham 2022.

Other big names include Canadian sprinter Andre De Grasse, who is a double Olympic champion, and two-time world 200m champion Shericka Jackson of Jamaica.

Meanwhile, Scotland's Eilish McColgan will be hoping to retain her Commonwealth title in the 10,000m.

Her triumph four years ago followed her mother Liz's victory in the same event at the 1986 Games in Edinburgh.

McColgan will line-up alongside compatriot Megan Keith, who broke the European 10k road record in Spain earlier this year.

Meanwhile, reigning 1500m Commonwealth champion Laura Muir will run the 5,000m.

In the pool, South Africa's Chad Le Clos was one of the biggest draws in 2014 after he defeated his hero, American legend Michael Phelps, in the 200m butterfly at the London Olympics.

Le Clos will return to Glasgow, where he won two golds in 2014, with the aim of becoming the most decorated male in the history of the Games by adding to his current haul of 18 medals.

Is Glasgow ready for the Commonwealth Games - and does the city care?

Hoy backs Glasgow 2026 for 'massive success'

Fellow Olympic champions competing at Tollcross include Mollie O'Callaghan of Australia, England's Adam Ramsay-Peaty and Daniel Wiffen of Northern Ireland.

Duncan Scott, Scotland's most successful Olympian, will hope to add to his collection of 13 Commonwealth medals.

But Archie Goodburn, who has terminal brain cancer, could provide one of the moments of the Games if he lands a podium finish in the 50m breaststroke.

Last month he told BBC Scotland: "I get goosebumps thinking about it."

Elsewhere, two five-time Commonwealth champions will be hoping to delight the home crowd.

Bowls great Alex "Tattie" Marshall will be making his eighth Games appearance and para-cycling star Neil Fachie will retire after riding both men's tandem events.

The mile returns to the Games for the first time since the 1966 edition in Kingston, Jamaica.

The race on 1 August - which will replace the 1500m - will see a stellar line-up go head-to-head over the distance made famous by Sir Roger Bannister.

In May 1954 he became the first person to run a mile in under four minutes and later that summer won gold over the same distance at the 1954 Games.

Fast forward 72 years and the line-up will include three Scots with high hopes of a medal.

Josh Kerr, fresh from breaking the 27-year-old mile world record in London, will be a favourite to top the podium.

But the former 1500m world champion will face competition from 2022 world champion Jake Wightman and Neil Gourley, who has world and European indoor 1500m medals to his name.

Australian track sensation Cameron Myers - who ran the fastest 1500m by an under-21, external in July - will also compete in the event.

In the women's mile race Scotland will be represented by Jemma Reekie.

The decision to scale back the Games meant there was some disappointment when the final schedule was announced.

Hockey was dropped despite a £5m national hockey centre being built for Glasgow 2014.

The cycling programme, which previously included mountain biking and road races, will be confined to the track.

In gymnastics, there is no room for any rhythmic events, with only artistic gymnastics being included.

Bowls, which were last time held at Kelvingrove, will be staged indoors at the SEC.

Badminton and squash were also scrapped and there will be no diving competitions.

In 2014 the baton relay visited 70 Commonwealth nations and territories over 248 days, covering a distance of 190,000km (118,060 miles).

The epic journey concluded with a 40-day trip across Scotland involving 4,000 baton bearers.

They included actor Brian Cox, Scotland manager Gordon Strachan, singer Susan Boyle, broadcaster Kirsty Wark and cycling legend Sir Chris Hoy.

This time round each competing team was given its own baton to decorate rather than transporting one across the Commonwealth.

The Scottish leg of the relay was launched on 1 July when swimmer Duncan Scott sailed up the River Clyde on a power boat.

Since then the baton has travelled to communities, events, and festivals across the country's 32 local authorities.

Celtic Park staged a spectacular opening ceremony in 2014 which featured Rod Stewart, dancing Tunnock's Tea Cakes and a Red Arrows fly over.

This year the event will be staged at the Hydro and will feature performances by headliner KT Tunstall, Callum Beattie, Nathan Evans, Tom Walker and Saint Phnx.

Inspiration for the opening ceremony has come from a variety of sources, including Bad Bunny's half-time show at this year's Superbowl.

Kylie Minogue, Lulu and Deacon Blue topped the bill for the last closing ceremony, which was staged at Hampden.

The curtain will come down on Glasgow 2026 at the Hydro after a ceremony featuring Delta Goodrem and rock legends Simple Minds.

Shankar Mahadevan, one of Bollywood's most renowned vocalists, and film star Rashmika Mandanna will also take to the stage to refelect the fact that the Indian city of Ahmedabad will host the centenary Games in October 2030.

Judo champion Sarah Adlington and Alex Marshall, who has won five Commonwealth gold medals in bowls, will lead out Team Scotland at the opening ceremony.

Adlington, one of Britain's most successful heavyweight judoka athletes, will bear the Scottish baton while Marshall will carry the Saltire flag.

Marshall said: "It will be an absolute privilege to lead this team out with Sarah, and I know every single one of us will give everything to bring success home for Team Scotland."

Adlington said: "Representing Scotland has always meant so much to me, so to have the opportunity to lead this team into a home Commonwealth Games is a real privilege that I'll cherish for the rest of my life."

The BBC was replaced by TNT Sports as live UK broadcast partner for the Games.

The subscription channel outbid the corporation, which had been the main partner and provided free-to-air coverage since 1954 for 18 games in-a-row.

Earlier this month it was confirmed BBC Alba is to screen nightly live coverage and highlights throughout the Games.

The Gaelic broadcaster will be the only place on the BBC where viewers can watch the action.

A similar deal was struck with S4C to provide live Welsh-language coverage.

Meanwhile, Channel 5 will screen the opening ceremony live and also run a nightly highlights show.

BBC Radio 5 Live is also providing extensive coverage of the Games featuring live event updates and daily evening broadcasts.

A cartoon thistle mascot was one of the stars of Glasgow 2014.

Fibreglass statues of Clyde - who was designed by 12-year-old Beth Gilmour - were dotted around the city and his beaming smile was all over social media.

Last July a unicorn called Finnie was unveiled as the official mascot for the 2026 Games.

But, earlier this month, Clyde staged a unlikely comeback as he joined Team Scotland.

Elinor Middlemiss, the team's chef de mission, said: "Clyde isn't just a mascot, he's part of Scotland's sporting story."

title: Crystal Palace sports centre set for £130m revamp
description: Bromley Council is to meet to discuss plans for a major revamp of the world famous sport centre.
fullText:
Plans to transform and regenerate the world-leading Crystal Palace National Sports Centre are set to be approved by Bromley Council.

The scheme include a revamp of the athletics stadium, indoor arena, and swimming and diving facilities.

The project - spearheaded by the Greater London Authority (GLA) and London's mayor Sir Sadiq Khan - is to be discussed by Bromley Council on Thursday, where planning officers have recommended it for approval.

Supporters have praised the sporting opportunities the new centre will bring to south London, while objectors are concerned certain sports could be disadvantaged under the new plans.

Bromley planners said the proposal would "significantly enhance the quality of the existing sports provision and improve the experience of users across both the borough and London".

The planning application has received hundreds of responses from Bromley residents; 390 people wrote in to support the project, while the proposal also garnered 406 objections.

The battle to bring London Diamond League 'home' to Crystal Palace

Supporters have welcomed the chance to bring the dilapidated complex back to life, with one describing it as "the beating heart of Crystal Palace, much loved and used".

Others felt the centre's revival would reduce crime and anti-social behaviour in the area by increasing sporting opportunities.

Several sporting bodies have also thrown their support behind the project, according to the Local Democracy Reporting Service (LDRS).

England Athletics said the project would "secure the long-term future of the site and allow it to serve as a modern, inclusive, and sustainable centre for athletics".

Volleyball England "strongly supports" the regeneration, it said.

Divers have said are keen to see the diving pools brought back into use and and water polo and underwater hockey players are hopeful the 50m pool could host matches and tournaments.

Sport England said there was "overwhelming support" for the plans.

A spokesperson said: "The Crystal Palace National Sports Centre is tired and dated, and in desperate need of investment and modernisation in order to make it inclusive and accessible to all sections of the local community and to bring it into the 21st century, as well as to set in sound financial footing for the future."

However, England Hockey and the Football Foundation have objected to the plans as they think the proposed shared multi-sport artificial grass pitch would not be sufficient for the demand that both hockey and football have.

The organisations are also concerned about the loss of an artificial pitch at the centre.

The indoor climbing community has voiced several concerns about the provision for a climbing wall within the new sports centre - which is to be moved to the current 5-a-side building.

Bromley planning officers argued that the space set aside for the climbing facility was nearly three times the size of the current one.

The proposal would mean some parts of the motorsport track are built on, something objectors say "prevents its use in the future and prevents people walking the historic track".

Bromley planning officers said the entirety of the racing circuit would be retained and remain "clearly recognisable" despite the changes.

Only some sections of Stadium Straight would be narrowed to enable the inclusion of additional outdoor sporting and recreational facilities, such as 5-a-side football and 3v3 basketball.

title: Do Manchester United need to explore CB options?
description: The 2026 FIFA World Cup came to an end on Sunday with Spain reclaiming the title. They won a gritty matchup against Argentina with a 1-0 victory after extra time, and the physicality in the contest led to the early departure of Manchester United defender Lisandro Martinez. The “Butcher” picked up the injury playing his […]
fullText:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup came to an end on Sunday with Spain reclaiming the title. They won a gritty matchup against Argentina with a 1-0 victory after extra time, and the physicality in the contest led to the early departure of Manchester United defender Lisandro Martinez.

The “Butcher” picked up the injury playing his game, an aggressive and fearless approach to defending that sees him cover a lot of ground and make a lot of crunching tackles. His efforts have made him a fan favorite at Old Trafford as one of the few transfer hits from the Erik ten Hag era. His tenure at Old Trafford has not come without bumps and bruises, though, and his absences with bumps and bruises have kept him from playing his best for extended stretches.

With his injury, even expected to be minor, there is some concern over Manchester United’s depth at the position. Matthijs De Ligt in particular was one fans were worried about after a lengthy absence and operation to fix a back injury. He is expected back in October, but has already had some injury problems hinder his career to this point. He and Martinez both missed a lot of time last season due to injury.

The notable absences are papered over somewhat by the enduring presence of Harry Maguire, who somehow rediscovered the confidence and ability that made him one of the club’s most expensive signings in 2019. He too has had his share of knocks and absences, though, and is turning 34 next March.

The presence of Leny Yoro and Ayden Heaven also papered over them. In fact, the immediate answer to the center back depth questions is this: That’s what Leny Yoro and Ayden Heaven are there for. That isn’t an answer that inspires a ton of confidence given the club experienced the reality of multiple injuries shattering the defensive unit in the last two seasons. But if the club is betting on a return to full health for De Ligt and a swift return for Martinez, there isn’t a pressing need for a center back.

The answer may lie somewhere between Heaven and Johnny Evans, who is now on the coaching staff under Michael Carrick. A shrewd move for a promising young defender or a veteran option who is fine playing in League Cup and emergencies. That sort of player would be useful in a campaign where the Reds transition from playing 40 games total to at least 48 games.

Among the free agent options with Premier League experience, John Stones seems unlikely given his history at Manchester City and the wages it would cost. Axel Tuanzebe could be an option, but at 28 he is likely a player looking for regular first-team football.

The club has done well in the market by identifying youth players on short or expiring deals, players the original clubs would like to keep. If there is a center back option there or rising in the ranks at Carrington, that seems to be the best option currently.

title: Aliff Sor Dechapan Receives Backing From Malaysian Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim Ahead Of World Title Fight On July 24
description: Before The Inner Circle 23 on Friday, hear what the Malaysian Prime Minister had to say about the young star!
fullText:
Malaysian Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim has rallied the nation behind Aliff Sor Dechapan, who stands one victory away from becoming his nation’s first-ever World Champion in ONE Championship.

Aliff will square off against the legendary Sam-A Gaiyanghadao for the ONE Interim Strawweight Muay Thai World Title in the main event of The Inner Circle 23, which goes down live on Friday, July 24.

The card will stream live in Asia primetime from Bangkok’s iconic Lumpinee Stadium, and it will be exclusively available to subscribers at live.onefc.com.

This is not the first time the Prime Minister has thrown his support behind one of Malaysia’s athletes in the world’s largest martial arts organization.

Anwar welcomed teenage knockout machine Johan “Jojo” Ghazali to the Prime Minister’s Office in Putrajaya last October, a meeting that underscored his backing for the country’s combat sports scene.

Now, that same support is being extended to Aliff, who stands on the precipice of becoming the first Malaysian ever to hold the coveted ONE World Title belt.

This is no small moment for a country whose presence in the global martial arts community continues to grow at a rapid pace.

Malaysia has produced a wave of promising talent in recent years, but a World Championship has remained just out of reach, making the 22-year-old’s opportunity inside Lumpinee Stadium a potential turning point for the bustling nation.

That significance has clearly not gone unnoticed at the highest levels of government. With the entire country watching, Anwar has chosen to lend his voice publicly in support of Aliff’s bid for history.

The Prime Minister took to his official Instagram account to voice his support for the young gun, urging fellow Malaysians to tune in for what could be a landmark sporting moment in the country.

“Let us rally behind Ahmad Nor Iman Aliff Rakib as he competes for the ONE Interim Strawweight Muay Thai World Championship title this Friday.

“Aliff Rakib has made history as the first Malaysian combat athlete to qualify for a shot at this title. May he continue to bring glory to Malaysia and inspire the younger generation to achieve success on a global stage.”

Riding that wave of national support, Aliff Sor Dechapan has made it clear his pursuit of gold extends well beyond personal glory.

For an athlete who has carried his country’s flag since debuting on the global stage in April 2023, this opportunity represents something far bigger than his own legacy.

Just days out from the biggest fight of his career, the Sor Dechapan representative made his motivation known to the entire nation watching.

The 22-year-old took to his Instagram account and wrote:

“I am not fighting for myself alone. I am fighting for my country. I have one dream – to bring the belt home and make history.

“I hope for the prayers and support of the entire nation. God willing, I will give my all to become the first fighter to bring that championship home for my country.”

Aliff heads into this monumental ONE Interim Strawweight Muay Thai World Title clash at The Inner Circle 23 on a five-bout winning streak.

Most recently, the Kuala Lumpur-based star earned the 62nd win of his career with a unanimous decision victory over Ramadan “The Scorpion” Ondash at ONE Fight Night 38 last December.

It also marked the ninth triumph of his promotional campaign, one filled with several knockouts against other world-class fighters.

Sam-A Gaiyanghadao represents his toughest test yet, but few warriors have looked this composed on the doorstep of history.

title: Saliba to miss extended period with back injury
description: Arsenal confirm defender William Saliba is set to miss an "extended period" after suffering a back injury while at the World Cup with France.
fullText:
Arsenal have confirmed defender William Saliba is set to miss an "extended period" after suffering a back injury while at the World Cup with France.

The 25-year-old had been managing a back issue for several weeks and carried the problem into the World Cup but was forced off after 30 minutes of France's semi-final loss to Spain.

After returning to London and being seen by a specialist, it was decided that surgery is not needed with a managed recovery programme to be commenced instead.

Saliba was forced to sit out France's World Cup bronze final defeat by England.

Earlier in the World Cup, he said: "I've had some minor niggles for several months.

"I've been gritting my teeth because there was the Champions League and the Premier League.

"The World Cup comes round only once every four years, so you've got to grit your teeth."

Saliba made 50 appearances across all competitions for Arsenal last season, as they ended a 22-year wait to win the Premier League and also reached the Champions League final.

Mikel Arteta's side will take on Manchester City in the Community Shield on Sunday, 16 August - five days before the Gunners begin the defence of the Premier League title, hosting promoted Coventry City.

title: Barcelona captain’s relationship with club deteriorates after serious World Cup injury – report
description: According to Catalunya Radio, relations between FC Barcelona and Frenkie de Jong have deteriorated significantly following the midfielder’s return from the FIFA World Cup with a severe knee injury. ...
fullText:
According to Catalunya Radio, relations between FC Barcelona and Frenkie de Jong have deteriorated significantly following the midfielder’s return from the FIFA World Cup with a severe knee injury.

Internal friction has mounted inside the Catalan club regarding how the physical setback occurred and how it was subsequently managed by the player and the Netherlands national team during the tournament.

The club officials are understood to be very angry and frustrated about the circumstances surrounding the player’s medical treatment while away on international duty.

Initial medical assessments conducted at the Ciutat Esportiva Joan Gamper indicate that the Dutch international will be sidelined for a period ranging between three to four months.

Out for 3-4 months. (Photo by Mike Hewitt/Getty Images)

While more tests are scheduled to take place in the coming days, early evaluations of De Jong’s right knee revealed significant instability and potential ligament damage, completely disrupting head coach Hansi Flick’s tactical planning ahead of the new season.

The club’s hierarchy believes the injury was severely exacerbated because the player pushed through intense discomfort to participate in the knockout rounds.

De Jong featured in pivotal matches while utilizing heavy bandaging and receiving painkilling injections, a medical approach that Barcelona’s staff feel compromised his long-term physical stability.

It is understandable that Barça are not at all happy with the developments, considering that De Jong already missed a chunk of matches last season due to injury and fitness issues.

De Jong’s injury has disrupted Flick’s plans. (Photo by Eric Alonso/Getty Images)

As per the report, a definitive decision regarding the exact clinical treatment pathway will be finalized on Friday, following a final round of specialised medical tests.

While the player’s camp has traditionally favoured conservative, non-invasive recovery methods to avoid the operating table, Barcelona’s institutional stance is clear.

The club is strongly pushing for immediate surgery to definitively resolve the underlying chronic instability in the knee joint and to ensure full healing and prevent subsequent relapses, even if it delays his competitive re-integration into the first-team squad until late autumn.

It remains to be seen what De Jong decides, with an official statement regarding his injury and recovery likely to arrive once the player chooses his next move.

title: Why are OpenAI and Anthropic cheering on regulation in Australia? The answer has global reach
description: The companies hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in...
fullText:
The companies hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in June

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Top US AI developers Anthropic and OpenAI cheered when Australia announced it would set new AI rules.

Big tech celebrating limits on their Silicon Valley VC-funded free-for-all might seem counterintuitive but there’s a much broader play than just what happens in one relatively small market.

Anthropic and OpenAI aren’t even on sharemarkets yet but this week lost billions in predicted value after being shown up by a Chinese startup.

Moonshot AI on Friday launched a new model, Kimi K3, advertising it as the first open-source model of its size, allowing more user modification than Anthropic’s Claude and OpenAI’s ChatGPT.

Analysts are judging K3 is competitive with both the leading US models, which are also less freely customisable. Moonshot had to temporarily pause new subscriptions after demand for the new model strained capacity.

Implied values for Anthropic’s sharemarket debut has slumped US$232bn to $1.56tn from Friday to Tuesday on IG’s trading platform. OpenAI dropped US$160bn to $1.16tn.

The two companies are still privately owned and valued at less than US$1tn each, so IG’s numbers are market bets, not true values.

But they hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in June.

Anthropic has already begun registering to list on US sharemarkets, which would allow it to raise billions from new investors. The better the story it can tell, the more money it can raise.

Chinese competition does not make for a good story. Nor do surprise lawsuits from unhappy creatives, such as the one Anthropic had to settle for US$1.5bn on Monday (Tuesday AEST).

The company will pay authors about US$3,000 for each of an estimated 500,000 books covered by the settlement.

One of the authors whose work Anthropic scraped was Andrew Charlton, Australia’s assistant technology minister. Charlton says the new regulation will mean Australian creatives can choose whether AI models are trained on their work and big tech companies will have to pay them.

Yet those new rules could be a boon to big tech, Charlton says.

“If we are upfront with what we expect in this Australian standard, we can give clarity to investors that in some ways enhances the attractiveness of Australia,” he told Sky News on Monday.

Malik Ahmed Khan, an equity analyst at Morningstar Equity, says technology companies would find it much easier to invest in Australia as they learn where regulation is heading.

The companies will also have an easier job convincing investors to buy in at high prices if they can show they’re focusing on building AI and managing the risk of running afoul of governments or local laws, Khan says.

While Australia is one of few countries to now be regulating AI directly, with little direct impact on companies based overseas, Anthony Albanese hopes to set the global tone. In his speech announcing the regulations, he cited Australia’s leadership on social media bans for under-16s as proof.

Khan says more countries following Australia’s lead on AI could boost to companies’ prospects and market values.

“A positive deal in Australia could enable them to pursue similar deals across the globe,” he said.

Regulation would also give companies the prospect of favourable terms.

Albanese signalled he would consult “closely” with industry and other countries to legislate regulations that would aim to enable AI’s arrival, not restrict it. OpenAI and Anthropic were quick to position themselves as partners ready to help shape the new approach.

Dario Amodei, Anthropic’s chief executive and a former OpenAI vice-president, has already given an indication of what big tech thinks it should look like.

In a June essay, he called for a coalition of democracies to dominate in AI and lock out China.

While there are real national security concerns in this area, there’s also a healthy dose of self-interest in blocking out competitors.

With the launch of Kimi’s K3, analysts believes it is increasingly likely that the US will block businesses from exclusively using Chinese-based models over national security fears.

Amodei also called for domestic regulation, asking governments to test, evaluate and even block AI models if they presented “unacceptable risks” – but with protections against local “political favoritism or arbitrary decisions”.

Anthropic’s feud with the Trump administration has left it on a US supply chain blacklist. Days after Amodei published his essay, the US government forced Anthropic to suspend access to its Mythos 5 models for foreign nationals.

Anthropic’s critics have accused it of self-interest as it nears its initial public offering (IPO) on sharemarkets. The Pentagon’s Kirsten Davies said in June: “Some things are simply more important than revenue cycles, clickbait and pre-IPO valuation.”

Yet Amodei’s warnings about security concerns have been echoed by Albanese and by Five Eyes intelligence agencies.

Aruna Sathanapally, the CEO of Australia’s Grattan Institute, met the Anthropic chief when he visited Canberra in April. She said Amodei’s concerns seemed sincere.

“He was seeing things within his own company that were giving him great concern about what Anthropic, as well as their competitors, may be capable of developing,” Sathanapally said.

“Just because they have vested interests in the particular shape of regulation, it doesn’t mean that the calls for regulation themselves are not well founded. It just means … you need to keep alive to competitive neutrality in how you design it.”

description:
