title: Splatoon Raiders review – Nintendo’s inky shooter fades to grey
description:
Nintendo Switch 2
The single-player offshoot of the quirky and colourful multiplayer series is disappointingly run-of-the-mill
Splatoon is among Nintendo’s most ingenious inventions of the last decade: a series of shooting games whose paint-splattered turf war nature means being bad at shooting is just as valuable as – if not more so than – being good. It is also blessed with an abundance of personality, with vibrant characters, a richly imagined world, and a hipness that the competition can only dream of achieving. Unfortunately, Splatoon Raiders makes it difficult to remember all of this.
The first spinoff in the ink-soaked franchise, Splatoon Raiders is advertised as a single player-focused entry in this primarily multiplayer series. While this is true enough – one can play through the entire game solo just fine – Raiders is clearly best enjoyed cooperatively with up to three others.
fullText:
Nintendo Switch 2
The single-player offshoot of the quirky and colourful multiplayer series is disappointingly run-of-the-mill

Splatoon is among Nintendo’s most ingenious inventions of the last decade: a series of shooting games whose paint-splattered turf war nature means being bad at shooting is just as valuable as – if not more so than – being good. It is also blessed with an abundance of personality, with vibrant characters, a richly imagined world, and a hipness that the competition can only dream of achieving. Unfortunately, Splatoon Raiders makes it difficult to remember all of this.

The first spinoff in the ink-soaked franchise, Splatoon Raiders is advertised as a single player-focused entry in this primarily multiplayer series. While this is true enough – one can play through the entire game solo just fine – Raiders is clearly best enjoyed cooperatively with up to three others. Players start each session on a hideout ship where they can upgrade their equipment and tweak their play style before heading out on bite-sized repeatable missions, and this structure is tailor-made for teaming up with friends or helping out strangers (the game lets you do either), as each person works towards creating their ultimate squid-kid avatar of chaos.

Playing Splatoon Raiders solo feels like a lesser experience for this reason. Its levels are straightforwardly constructed to keep groups together. There is little of the puzzly rush of finding hidden depths with creative use of your tools. Instead, the fun is in putting together your kit. Splatoon’s weapons have always been goofy and playfully designed, but even better is learning how this new entry complements them with a suite of augmentable gadgets. As your options accrete and the game escalates in challenge, Raiders comes to life.

Some may not get that far. Splatoon Raiders takes a long time to make it clear how completely the experience can change with the right kit, and while it offers a decent variety of challenges to tackle, players are meant to replay them. The minimal story, in which you are an unnamed mechanic stranded on a mysterious archipelago with the band Deep Cut (returning from Splatoon 3), is not terribly compelling. Which is surprising for a series with a history of casually revealing outrageous new details about its post-climate apocalypse world and its human-cephalopod inhabitants.

Splatoon is Nintendo at its most offbeat, venturing forth in a direction few other game studios travel. And, according to sales, audiences outside of the company’s native Japan seem to struggle with how to receive it. In this context, Splatoon Raiders feels like a concession: Nintendo taking aside one of its rangier, peculiar creations and asking it to be a bit more normal. This is the biggest surprise about Splatoon Raiders: it is pedestrian. If you’ve played one of dozens of modern cooperative action games, you’ve played this. You have upgraded your gear and compared weapon stats and divined synergies between abilities in order to throw your character into the meat grinder over and over again, watching overwhelming hordes eventually become trivial as your stats improve, your arsenal more varied. There is only so much that Splatoon’s ink-fuelled weaponry and charming DIY design sensibilities can do to elevate this experience. Like its archipelago setting, Splatoon Raiders is far removed from what makes the main series so charming, and what’s left here feels a bit too much like watching paint dry.

Splatoon Raiders is out 23 July; £41.99

title: Headaches for Silicon Valley as China chips away at the US’s lead in the AI race
description:
Google’s AI struggles scream trouble as new Chinese models (again) throw US tech dominance into question
Hello, I’m Blake Montgomery, writing to you after a double-header feature of Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey and the World Cup final. What a great Sunday. Today in tech, we’re discussing how China is chipping away at the US’s lead in the AI race and how Silicon Valley’s workers are taking action to protect their jobs from AI.
New York becomes first state to impose one-year pause on new AI datacenters
Trump rails against New York’s statewide datacenter moratorium
Albanese’s AI blueprint sparks calls for datacentre moratorium until new regulations in place
‘Adversarial clothing’: are garments designed to confuse facial recognition systems about to go mainstream?
IBM loses quarter of its value as tech giant’s shares plunge and profits falter
‘What’s the point?
fullText:
Google’s AI struggles scream trouble as new Chinese models (again) throw US tech dominance into question

Hello, I’m Blake Montgomery, writing to you after a double-header feature of Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey and the World Cup final. What a great Sunday. Today in tech, we’re discussing how China is chipping away at the US’s lead in the AI race and how Silicon Valley’s workers are taking action to protect their jobs from AI.

Google is struggling with the latest version of its Gemini model

 A Chinese company, Moonshot, put out a free, surprisingly capable model last week

 China’s new models yet again thrown the US dominance in AI into question

Google is months behind in its launch schedule for the new version of its flagship AI model, Gemini 3.5. According to Bloomberg, Google is attempting to improve Gemini’s coding abilities to catch up to Claude, the Anthropic bot that is widely considered the best AI coding tool. OpenAI released a new model at the start of July; Google had been expected to do the same in May. News of the delay sent Google’s shares sliding as investors questioned whether it could maintain its top position in the AI race.

A refreshed and newly capable Gemini might also have ameliorated the damage to the US’s reputation as the leader in AI this week – but didn’t.

As Google’s internal struggles became public, the Chinese startup Moonshot debuted an unexpectedly strong model, sending shivers through Silicon Valley and Washington DC alike. Moonshot’s Kimi K3 model featured surprisingly capable coding, particularly in a niche called “front-end coding”. “This may be the single biggest release of the year,” Anastasios Angelopoulos, the CEO of AI testing company Arena, said.

Unlike ChatGPT, Claude and Gemini, Kimi K3 is free to use and open-weight, meaning anyone can download it and run it locally on their own computers, as its core components are released to the public.

It’s been the general strategy of Chinese AI companies: open-source and open-weight models, beating US AI companies on price as Chinese manufacturers do, and mirroring the broader trade war of the US and China. DeepSeek inspired a major US market panic last year. Alibaba previewed its latest model, Qwen3.8 Max, on Sunday, showing off a similar number of parameters as Kimi K3. The company plans to publish the model’s weights soon, per Bloomberg.

The strategy has been made possible by Beijing, which subsidizes computing resources and power consumption for its domestic AI startups, allowing them to release their AI models for free. The basic logic goes that if these startups can’t compete on capability with US models, they can compete on cost and undercut the US parent companies, which need to earn a profit eventually.

It’s all causing major headaches in Silicon Valley. There are no longtime incumbents in the AI business to whom customers will default. If those buyers, especially businesses who need lots of generated code, can turn to cheap or free alternatives, there might be little need to spend big on brand-name bots. The South China Morning Post, which is itself owned by the Alibaba Group, reported last week: “As US AI costs soar, global businesses pivot to China’s low-cost, open-weight models.”

In the US, nerves are rattling. Hawkish Trump administration officials are rekindling their efforts to block any US access to Chinese AI models via de facto bans, commerce department rules or an executive order, Axios reported Monday.

OpenAI’s policy head for advanced AI, former Donald Trump adviser Dean Ball, wrote that Beijing’s open-weight approach to AI would lead to “full AI communism” with government-run datacenters powering AI as a public good. He denounced that future. OpenAI has been sounding the alarm about Chinese models for years, which reads as part political disagreement and part business strategy, since Chinese open-weight models threaten OpenAI’s projected profits.

“I would guess that the Trump Administration will at some point realize that their best strategy here would be to create large amounts of regulatory risk around the use of open-weight Chinese models,” Ball wrote.

There is no alignment between the US’s leading AI companies and the government players in the same industry. Emil Michael, the US Pentagon’s chief technology officer, responded to Ball: “Every industry/ecosystem has its supreme village idiot. Dean Ball is that for AI.”

As Beijing presents a unified front, the White House’s response is growing more scattered by the day. Trump himself had talked until recently of complete deregulation and never restraining any AI investment, research or releases. By contrast, the White House is restricting access – even allies’ access – to Anthropic and OpenAI’s advanced cybersecurity models. Even the personnel are changing. Chris Fall, the director of the Trump administration’s Center for AI Standards and Innovation, is resigning just three months into his tenure, Axios reported Monday.

Not all of the AI crowd is against these models’ incursion on US dominance. Bill Gurley, a longtime venture capitalist best known for his early backing of Uber, wrote of China’s AI models in the Washington Post: “They aren’t a security threat – they’re what competition looks like.”

China’s advanced AI does pose a risk to the global business of AI, which is, as yet, still a race without a clear winner. Cheap Chinese AI models may trump their expensive US counterparts. The entire US economy is tied tight to the AI industry, raising the stakes of the Chinese models’ capabilities. Undercutting the US’s AI frontrunners’ business could unsettle the entire US stock market even more so than it already has.

That competition is set to play out in the future. In the present, though, China’s recent releases have thrown the politics of AI in the US into chaos. That reads as the bigger win.

New York becomes first state to impose one-year pause on new AI datacenters

Trump rails against New York’s statewide datacenter moratorium

Albanese’s AI blueprint sparks calls for datacentre moratorium until new regulations in place

Employees are taking some of the US’s first collective actions to protect their jobs from AI

Last week, Meta workers filed a lawsuit against their former employer alleging that it automated their firing via algorithmic decision-making. Meanwhile, thousands of Google’s workers petitioned their CEO for layoff protections as the company shifts its spending from employees to AI infrastructure.

“Make no mistake: this is a company that is enjoying massive, unprecedented success,” Parul Koul, a Google software engineer and the president of the Alphabet Workers Union, said outside the company’s California headquarters. “These layoffs and cuts are not difficult decisions, but simply profit being put over the people that make this company run.”

These Silicon Valley employees are making these moves against the backdrop of the AI boom, which is changing software engineering rapidly and causing unions to gain popularity.

A Meta employee who was part of a recent union drive in the UK said: “It’s about us having a voice in all of the operations of the company, from products to employee experience.”

Meta countered last week’s lawsuit with an unusually direct statement: “These claims lack merit and are not based on facts. Workforce management and organizational decisions were and are made by people, not AI.” Google did not issue a statement in response to last week’s petition.

We’ve seen Silicon Valley luminaries warn of futuristic apocalyptic scenarios, but we’re seeing those worries come to life among the fearful rank-and-file employees, who are now taking action. We’re witnessing how those workforces, who have a front-row seat to AI’s development, respond to its effects on their employment.

Read more from Reworked, a Guardian series about what’s at stake as AI disrupts our jobs.

IBM loses quarter of its value as tech giant’s shares plunge and profits falter

‘What’s the point?’ Teenagers give their verdict on Britain’s social media curfew

Amazon Web Services customers receive bills for up to $1.5tn after global glitch

Trump made $1.4bn from crypto in one year. Is Justin Sun the man who helped him do it?

title: Inside the horrifying online network where children extort others to commit violence and self-harm
description:
The 764 network is just one part of a growing online ecosystem that pushes young members into brutal and degrading acts. It has perpetrators and victims in dozens of countries
In late 2024, in a quiet suburb of Stockholm, a 14-year-old boy approached a man in his 80s who was using a walker. He stabbed the man three times in the back.
The video of the attack in Hässelby was streamed and spread quickly. It fed a global online ecosystem that actively encourages and glorifies acts of violence and degradation – the “gamification of harm”, as the researcher Marc-André Argentino puts it.
fullText:
In late 2024, in a quiet suburb of Stockholm, a 14-year-old boy approached a man in his 80s who was using a walker. He stabbed the man three times in the back.

The video of the attack in Hässelby was streamed and spread quickly. It fed a global online ecosystem that actively encourages and glorifies acts of violence and degradation – the “gamification of harm”, as the researcher Marc-André Argentino puts it.

The boy allegedly had links to a network known as 764. Such groups are known to target young people in games including Roblox and Minecraft as well as communities struggling with mental health or sexual identity for online extortion, pressuring them into acts of violence and self-harm.

The 764 network, founded by a Texas teenager, Bradley Cadenhead, is just one part of an evolving online ecosystem, with perpetrators and victims in dozens of countries. Cadenhead is now serving an 80-year sentence in the US for possessing child sexual abuse material.

There are several cases currently before courts around the world with alleged links to such groups. Legal records from numerous countries suggest they often celebrate violence, manipulation and harm for its own sake, collecting records of such acts to gain notoriety and control.

“These are young people recruiting other young people,” says Bjørn Ihler, the founder of the security firm Revontulet. “It is a youth phenomenon.”

The Australian federal police commissioner, Krissy Barrett, has said: “A worrying trend is how victims are becoming perpetrators to try to escape their online bullies who have befriended and groomed them, and then forced them to hurt themselves.”

A Swedish court decided in April 2025 that the teenager had carried out the stabbing although he was too young to be held criminally responsible. But another name emerged during the investigation, local authorities told the Guardian – an online username: “Chai.”

In early 2025, as the country was still reeling from the attack, an Australian girl rang Swedish police. She wanted to tell authorities about Chai.

Linus Svensson, an investigative reporter at the Swedish broadcaster SVT, had never heard of 764 before the 2024 stabbing.

Svensson says he now knows of at least nine local court cases related to “the Com” – the term for a broad online network that engages in cybercrime, extortion and violence, and which includes 764. He says he has found dozens of Swedish members in chat groups.

“It’s bigger than these few cases,” he says. “I think it took a while for Swedish authorities and also the general public to actually understand this.”

As he began to wade through videos and imagery shared by the group for SVT’s investigation, Svensson came across a teenager in the north of Sweden who had been convicted in 2024 of trying to coerce a 13-year-old boy to take his own life.

This, according to Jenny Östling, a senior public prosecutor with Sweden’s National Unit against International and Organised Crime, was Chai.

The teenager, whose real name cannot be published, is on trial for more than 70 alleged offences, according to the indictment, including allegedly encouraging someone to self-harm and kill themselves, and the creation of child sexual abuse material. Most were allegedly committed against the Australian girl.

He is also charged with inducing a young man in Germany to harm himself so badly he almost died.

His lawyer, Emily Töyrä, told the Guardian he denies all the allegations.

The trial was paused in late June after the Swedish court sent him for a forensic psychiatric investigation, according to court records, stating there was “compelling evidence that [he] has committed several of the acts with which he has been charged”. The matter will return in August.

Östling says the case will test whether people can be held responsible for remotely coercing others into acts of self-harm or violence – a hallmark of groups such as 764.

“They have a goal for the group itself: to destroy the world … But [members] also have a goal for themselves: they want to gain status within the group,” Östling says.

Creating new material can be a prerequisite of entry to closed chatrooms and other markers of membership. “They primarily coerce young victims into cutting themselves or sexual acts,” she says. “They use it to be able to coerce them into doing something worse.”

Östling alleges Chai was “high status” within 764, and was invited into a chatroom known as “764 Inferno”.

His lawyer disputes that characterisation, and says her client’s involvement was limited to editing images and videos. “The Com environment is highly fluid,” Töyrä says. “My client does not accept the characterisation of ‘764 Inferno’ as the type of organised group alleged by the prosecution. In his view, it was simply one group chat among many.”

An indictment unveiled by the US Department of Justice in April 2025 alleges that two American men in their early 20s, Leonidas Varagiannis and Prasan Nepal, were part of a chatroom with the same name which “was reserved for the inner core of 764 members”.

The pair are accused of using “764 Inferno” to conspire with other members to create and distribute child exploitation images, among other charges. Researchers have found at least 295 arrests linked to the Com network in 33 countries, including Australia, where there are matters before the courts.

In New Zealand, after a tipoff from US authorities, Ronndog Keefe pleaded guilty in 2025 to 13 charges, including the distribution of “horrific and graphic” child exploitation material. The 22-year-old was sentenced to more than five years’ jail in December.

Keefe claimed he was also a victim of 764, which he said had threatened to dox him if he did not distribute such material, according to legal records, but the court found this “simply was not credible”.

Last year in the UK, Cameron Finnigan, then 19, was given a nine-year extended sentence for possessing a terrorist document – a “kill guide” with advice about how to commit “truck attacks” – and indecent images of children. He was also convicted of encouraging someone online to take their own life.

The court found Finnigan was part of 764. “The intended victims are … vulnerable individuals: in particular, LGBTQI+ youth, racial minorities and those suffering from mental illnesses or disorders,” the judge said at his sentencing. “They are targeted precisely because they are so obviously vulnerable.”

In June another British teenager, Elijah Palmer, was sentenced for encouraging serious self-harm, among other charges. He was also linked to 764, police said, and had targeted his victim on Roblox and the chat platform Discord.

The FBI uses the term “nihilistic violent extremism” to describe these activities but there is debate about the moniker. Some argue it is vague and tries to unite too many different groups under one umbrella.

Researchers such as Argentino have noted that ideology in these spaces can be “fluid, performative, and primarily symbolic” – occult or neo-Nazi imagery can function as markers of group membership rather than “as coherent political programs”.

Membership is also vague, according to Bjørn Ihler, who prefers to add the term “misanthropic”. “These are basically Discord servers,” he says. “There’s channels upon channels upon channels.”

Ultimately, the pursuit of sextortion material is about “power and … control of the victim”, Ihler says, and such content can be shared in so-called “lore books” for further victimisation by others.

“Sharing those materials … leads to increased ‘clout’, which is like the social currency,” he says. “Grappling with that is incredibly important from a victim-protection perspective.”

Long before these arrests took place, an independent researcher, Becca Spinks, had been tracking these groups from her home in Texas.

“As a mom, I’m just horrified,” she says. “I couldn’t close my eyes and just be like, ‘Oh, well, someone else will deal with it.’ Who’s working on this? What’s going on? How can we help?”

As part of her work, she started to speak to the mother of Chai’s Australian alleged victim several years ago, who was looking for help to get the attention of authorities and assistance for her child.

The trials of men linked to 764 taking place all over the world, Spinks says, have come as some relief. “She’s very brave,” she says of the alleged victim in Chai’s case. “And her mum also is really just a powerhouse.”

Chai’s trial has largely taken place behind closed doors but the toll the alleged crimes have taken on victims is clear.

Spinks says her biggest priority is raising awareness for parents and law enforcement officials.

“A lot of times kids come forward and maybe [the police] don’t recognise the signs that it’s the 764 network, and so they don’t pursue it as aggressively as they should,” she says.

Spinks says there needs to be greater awareness for parents too. The Australian federal police cite warning signs including indications of self-harm, sudden withdrawal from family and friends and secretive behaviour about online interactions.

“One of the biggest, most dangerous misunderstandings is that this won’t happen to us, or this only happens to a certain type of kid, when in reality, it could honestly happen to anyone.”

Do you know more? Contact ariel.bogle@theguardian.com

In Australia, support is available at Beyond Blue on 1300 22 4636, Lifeline on 13 11 14, and at MensLine on 1300 789 978. In the UK, the charity Mind is available on 0300 123 3393 and Childline on 0800 1111. In the US, call or text Mental Health America at 988 or chat 988lifeline.org.

title: Colorado's socialist candidate calls for 'more immigrants into office' to stop America's 'exploitative' nature
description: Colorado congressional candidate Melat Kiros called for more immigrants to enter public office and challenge America's exploitative nature on the global stage.
fullText:
Colorado socialist candidate Melat Kiros pushed more immigrants with "heritage in the global south" to enter office and push back against the "exploitative and extractive" nature of the United States last week.

Kiros took part in the Wa'Ela Tigrai event in Denver, Colorado, last weekend and discussed her rising congressional campaign after defeating longtime incumbent Rep. Diana DeGette, D-Colo., during a Democratic primary last month.

Drawing on the experiences of House "Squad" members like Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, D-N.Y., Kiros spoke about the importance of helping others to achieve after her for the sake of changing the global world order. One way, she argued, was pushing people who understand American exploitation "on a fundamental level."

Colorado Socialist Candidate Called 9/11 Terror Attacks 'Inevitable' Due To Us Foreign Policy

"There's a certain reckoning that we are going to have to have with the global world order as it exists today and the way that it fundamentally will have to shift going forward," Kiros said. "The responsibility that we have as Americans, the recognition that is going to be required of us, of the exploitative and extractive nature that we play on the global scale and the global stage is only going to be challenged if we are getting represented by people who understand that on a fundamental level."

She continued, "And that means getting more immigrants into office. That means getting more people who have heritage in the global south into office because that's where those decisions are made specifically."

Fox News Digital reached out to Kiros' campaign for comment.

Socialism's Rise Inside The Democratic Party Now Threatens The American Dream

Kiros was one of several candidates endorsed by the Democratic Socialists of America (DSA) who defeated an incumbent Democratic politician in a primary election for the 2026 midterms.

During the event, she appeared to push an anti-capitalist sentiment based on her experience as an attorney, claiming that it "broke [her] spirit."

"When I was in law school, I tell everybody that was where I learned that the system is not broken, that it's actually working exactly as it was designed to. And that really kind of broke my spirit a little bit and made me believe there's really nothing to be done, which is why I went to a big law firm in the first place and just wanted to retire and take care of my parents and call it a day. But that's no way to live, right? At the end of the day, the spirit of our people, the spirit of our community and our culture is to fight and to never give up," Kiros said.

Socialist Colorado Candidate Blames Israel For 'Putting Jewish People In Great Danger'

She added, "Regardless of what ends up happening, regardless of what happens with my own candidacy and this general and then what this potential, you know, office could look like with me in it… I would rather go down fighting than to give up."

Kiros was previously fired from her position at the Sidley Austin law firm in 2023 after publishing an open letter criticizing law firms, including her own, that called for action against antisemitism on college campuses.

title: Tottenham sign France defender Sombath from Lyon
description: Tottenham have signed France international Alice Sombath for a club record fee from Lyon.
fullText:
Tottenham have signed France international defender Alice Sombath for a club record transfer fee from Lyon.

The 22-year-old joins for an undisclosed fee and the length of her contract is unknown.

The previous club record signing is believed to have been Norway international Signe Gaupset's arrival for around £375,000 last year.

Sombath has spent five seasons at European giants Lyon, making 102 appearances and breaking into the France senior squad.

During her time in France, she won five consecutive Premiere Ligue titles and was part of the successful 2021-22 Women's Champions League squad.

Promoted Crystal Palace sign striker England

"I want to do my best here at Tottenham. I think the league is very competitive, every game is tough and that's what I'm most excited about," said Sombath.

"I saw that Spurs had a very good season last year. My qualities can bring something to the team, even though I know they already have a lot."

Sombath came through Paris FC's academy and joined Paris St-Germain's youth team set-up before her move to Lyon in 2020.

She can play at centre-back or right-back and has represented France at every age group from the under-16s to the senior squad.

The defender featured in all of France's matches at Euro 2025, starting three games in the group stages.

"She's a player we've followed closely for some time and one who fits the profile of what we're building here," said Tottenham manager Martin Ho.

"She has already shown real maturity in her performances, and we believe she has all the qualities to continue developing into a top player within our environment.

"Alice is an aggressive defender who is comfortable in possession, competitive in her duels, and has the ambition to keep improving every day.

"This is another important addition as we continue building for the future. We can't wait to get started with Alice and welcome her into the group."

Tottenham have been successful in the pursuit of their key targets during the transfer window, bringing in six additions including Sombath.

They have also signed Shekeira Martinez, Kirsty Hanson, Victoria Pelova, Caitlin Dijkstra and Selma Panengstuen, getting their business done early.

title: Bournemouth midfielder Scott rejects new contract
description: Bournemouth midfielder Alex Scott rejects a new contract amid growing Premier League interest in the England Under-21 international.
fullText:
Bournemouth midfielder Alex Scott has rejected a new contract amid interest from rival Premier League clubs in the England Under-21 international.

The Cherries made it a summer priority to tie Scott down to fresh terms, but as things stand there is no agreement.

Arsenal, Manchester United, Tottenham, Manchester City and Chelsea have an interest in Scott.

BBC Sport reported in June that Arsenal had an approach for Scott turned down by Bournemouth.

The 22-year-old, who joined Bournemouth from Bristol City in 2023 for £25m, has two years left on his current deal with the Premier League side.

Bournemouth remain steadfast in their position that Scott is not for sale, despite him rejecting the contract offer that had been on the table for at least two months.

Bournemouth have insisted publicly, directly to the player and to interested clubs that he will remain on the south coast, even on his current contract.

That stance has been consistent and, despite talk of an £80m valuation, they have continued to reject approaches.

The south coast club are under no pressure to sell after qualifying for the Europa League and generating significant income through the sales last summer of Dean Huijsen, Milos Kerkez, Illia Zabarnyi and Dango Outtara.

They have taken a similar stance with highly rated forward Junior Kroupi, who is attracting interest from Arsenal and Tottenham.

Brazil international Rayan is also not expected to leave and has given no indication that he wants to, having only joined the club in January.

Why are Premier League transfer fees soaring?

How the World Cup has affected your Premier League team

Scott and Kroupi are with the squad for new manager Marco Rose's pre-season training camp in Austria, with Rayan due back at a later date having played for Brazil at the World Cup.

Scott was selected in his first England squad last November and was then called into Thomas Tuchel's extended pre-World Cup training camp in Florida but is yet make an appearance at senior international level.

In a busy Premier League midfielder market this summer, Nottingham Forest's Elliot Anderson and Aston Villa's Morgan Rogers are set to complete moves to Manchester City and Chelsea for £116m and £117m respectively.

Tottenham have spent £100m on Newcastle's Sandro Tonali and £85m on West Ham's Mateus Fernandes.

Scott joined Bristol City from Guernsey FC in 2020, going on to make 91 appearances for the Championship side in all competitions.

Having been named the division's young player of the season in 2022-23, he joined Bournemouth where he has made 89 appearances and scored six goals.

He helped Bournemouth finish sixth in 2025-26 under Andoni Iraola, qualifying for European football for the first time in their history.

title: Keeper Martinez may 'step aside' from Argentina
description: Argentina goalkeeper Emi Martinez says he is considering whether it is "time to step aside" from international football after his side's 1-0 defeat to Spain in the World Cup final.
fullText:
Argentina goalkeeper Emi Martinez says he is considering whether it is "time to step aside" from international football after his side's 1-0 defeat to Spain in the World Cup final.

The 33-year-old has won 67 caps for his country and played every minute of Argentina's eight World Cup games, keeping two clean sheets and conceding eight goals.

He was a World Cup winner in 2022, also claiming the golden glove award as the tournament's outstanding goalkeeper.

"I dreamt we'd win it again, I dreamt of bringing it back to Argentina and making history once more," Martinez wrote in a post on Instagram., external

"The truth is, the pain is hard to explain. There are many things to reflect on and how to move forward, and if it's time to step aside.

"I'm so sorry, I truly tried my best to help my country and my team-mates."

Martinez won the Yashin Trophy, awarded to the world's best goalkeeper at the Ballon d'Or awards, in 2023 and 2024.

He has played for Aston Villa since 2020 and has a contract until 2029, but has been linked to a move to Italian side Juventus this summer.

title: Why World Cup can be game-changer for ambitious MLS
description: Can the success of the 2026 World Cup help MLS become one of the world's elite leagues?
fullText:
"Thanks world, We'll take it from here."

That's the message from Lionel Messi, Son Heung-min, David Beckham and dozens of Major League Soccer players in an advertising campaign urging new football fans created by the World Cup to check out MLS.

The slick film showcases World Cup stars who play in the US top flight, inviting fans to join them as the regular season resumes. The league is even offering free tickets to new fans.

"The World Cup has now exposed millions of people to Major League Soccer," says MLS executive vice-president Dan Courtemanche, who joined the league in 1995 before a ball was kicked.

"We're going to continue to capitalise on that momentum."

Could the lasting legacy of this World Cup be the rise of MLS into the band of elite leagues?

Major League Soccer is itself a legacy product from the 1994 World Cup, when Fifa mandated the US to establish a professional league as part of its bid to host the tournament.

The league started in 1996 with 10 teams and zero purpose-built stadiums. Thirty years later, MLS has 30 clubs and almost as many football-specific stadiums.

Despite predictions of empty World Cup seats because of sky-high ticket prices, the stadiums were packed, and broadcast ratings were impressive as football joy spread across the United States - even after the USA were knocked out of the tournament.

The US team's loss to Belgium at the last-16 stage delivered the most-watched football game on one network in US history - with more than 33 million watching on Fox.

England's last-16 win over Mexico became the most-watched football game in US history with a combined 45 million viewers in English and Spanish watching across Fox and Telemundo.

The ratings compare with games in the NFL - what Americans consider football - but are not up to the level of the showpiece Super Bowl.

In 2025, the most-watched Super Bowl in history attracted 127.7 million viewers across English and Spanish broadcasts and streaming in the US, although many Americans tune in solely for the half-time show, which was performed by Kendrick Lamar that year.

MLS, which has shown strong growth since its first season 30 years ago, aspires to become an elite league on par with Europe's top competitions.

"I think we're looked at as the top, one of the top leagues commercially," MLS commissioner Don Garber said at an MLS event to discuss the future of the league and the post-World Cup legacy. "We've cracked that code. Now we've got to crack the rest of it."

In some ways, MLS is already up there with the big leagues across the world.

There were 45 MLS players represented at the 2026 World Cup - including two in the final for Argentina, Messi and Rodrigo de Paul.

MLS has a reputation for being almost a retirement home for superstars - a lucrative place to play for legends like Beckham and Messi after peak careers in Europe.

But that reputation is outdated, Courtemanche says, with the average age of new international players in the league now 23.8 years. Though many of them have dreams of playing in Europe after experiencing MLS.

MLS is changing its calendar in 2027 to adopt a summer-to-spring season, more aligned with Europe to make it easier for players to move between the leagues.

And the competition's leaders are investing in youth development leagues.

All MLS teams run youth academies. As LA Galaxy hosted a viewing party for the US victory over Bosnia-Herzegovina on a big screen, dozens of children ignored the match, instead battling it out themselves on three separate pitches, occasionally running to the big screen when the sound of a goal spread through the crowd.

These were rare pick-up games in a culture full of highly scheduled children - and the skill level was high. Some of the kids played for MLS youth teams.

Eight-year-old Landon - named after US star Landon Donovan - was one of the more skilled, moving from goalkeeper to forward with glee. His mother Jen Wing is a coach and says her children have attended MLS camps and play on travel teams at their home in New Jersey.

Landon dreams of being a professional soccer player one day in MLS.

"LAFC," he says, referring to his dream team.

But critics say MLS will never compete with Europe until it sorts out its "pay-to-play" culture in youth development.

At the top level, MLS academies train and develop youth players for free, much like their European counterparts.

But to get to that level, where an MLS academy team would notice a teenage player, families typically pay thousands of dollars for private lessons and access to travel teams, a common theme in US sports.

MLS officials say the development leagues are constantly evolving and improving when it comes to developing young talent, and that every team has scholarships available to talented players.

Commissioner Garber says the league is always improving and is determined to get even better at developing young players so they can deliver "more and more to the men's national team and just be better, bigger, stronger, faster, all those things that give you energy to think of a better future".

When it comes to attracting audiences, Messi is the league's biggest draw.

But the cult of celebrity runs throughout the league. More than two dozen entertainers and athletes hold ownership stakes in MLS clubs, including Will Ferrell (LAFC), Reese Witherspoon (Nashville SC), Matthew McConaughey (Austin FC) and Magic Johnson (LAFC).

Drew Carey was an original Seattle Sounders owner when the club joined MLS in 2009 for a $30m (£22m) expansion fee. The Sounders are now valued at more than $900m (£668m). David Beckham's 2007 LA Galaxy contract included the option to buy an MLS expansion team for $25m. He now owns Inter Miami CF, a club that experts have valued at more than a billion dollars.

But as much as the US is experiencing a football frenzy right now, many believe US culture will always prioritise American football.

Brian Udovich, whose 12-year-old son Croil plays in an MLS Next Level league in Southern California, says many talented youngsters leave the sport for American football when they become teenagers because of all the fanfare.

On Friday nights, American football players get their names up in lights with marching bands and cheerleaders urging them on to glory in giant stadiums. Whereas soccer is typically played on Saturday mornings with mums and dads in folding chairs.

"It just means so much more to other cultures than us," Udovich says.

Michael Bradley, coach of the New York Red Bulls, says American culture is just different and that the legacy of the World Cup will make association football more prominent.

"We don't have the long, storied football history that other countries do. I think for a long time that led to an inferiority complex that maybe football would never really survive in the United States," Bradley told the BBC, adding that now so many more fans in the US feel proud.

"I think we can all unequivocally say in 2026, football is not going anywhere."

title: Report: Man United are leading the race to sign World Cup midfielder
description: Manchester United Hold Early Edge in Manu Kone Transfer RaceManchester United appear to be in a promising position as the market for Manu Kone begins to develop, with L’Equipe reporting that the Fra...
fullText:
Manchester United appear to be in a promising position as the market for Manu Kone begins to develop, with L’Equipe reporting that the France international is giving serious thought to the next step in his career.

Kone, now 25, comes into this summer at an important moment. After two solid seasons with Roma, where he has made 82 appearances, and after helping the Italian side secure third place with 73 points last term, the midfielder has strengthened his standing. His World Cup ended painfully with France’s 2-0 semi-final defeat to Spain, and the emotional aftermath underlined how deeply he had invested in the campaign. Yet that tournament also seems to have reinforced the sense that he is ready for a bigger platform.

The central point is clear. Kone wants to test himself at a higher level, ideally with a club carrying strong European ambitions. Inter Milan have tracked the situation for some time, according to reporting in Italy, but the strongest likelihood now appears to be a move to the Premier League.

Among the English clubs with interest, Manchester United are described as having something of a head start. Initial talks have taken place, although the situation has not yet moved into advanced territory. That reflects a wider theme in this summer window, with clubs across Europe operating carefully after a market slowed by the World Cup.

United’s recruitment team are understood to be assessing a number of midfield options, and Kone is firmly among the priority names on that shortlist. The club’s stance is measured. There is a determination to avoid overpaying and to weigh up each move properly before accelerating. In that context, interest should be taken seriously, but not yet viewed as definitive.

From Kone’s side, there is believed to be genuine openness to Manchester United. The attraction of English football has been there for some time, and his current market is strongest in the Premier League. Arsenal have also been mentioned previously, while two other English sides are said to have expressed interest, even if their identities remain undisclosed.

The player would prefer clarity sooner rather than later, particularly after a demanding international summer. Whether that arrives quickly is another matter. For now, Manchester United seem well placed, but this remains a transfer race still in its early stages.

As a Manchester United supporter, this is exactly the sort of report that grabs the attention. Manu Kone feels like a player who fits what the squad has lacked in midfield for too long, energy, athleticism, intensity and the ability to cover ground without sacrificing quality on the ball. If the club are serious about refreshing the engine room, he looks like a very sensible target.

What stands out most is his apparent openness to the move. That matters. United have spent too many windows chasing players who needed convincing, and that rarely ends well. If Kone sees Old Trafford as the right next step, then the club should recognise that opportunity and act decisively.

At the same time, there is some reassurance in the idea that United do not want to rush or overpay. Supporters have seen too many expensive deals made from panic rather than planning. A calmer process is welcome, as long as it does not become hesitation for its own sake.

From a football perspective, Kone could bring balance and drive. He looks ready for the Premier League, and his age suggests there is still another level to come. If United want midfielders who can compete physically, progress the ball and grow into major roles, this feels like one worth pushing hard for.

title: Lionel Messi’s Wife Sends Heartfelt Message After World Cup Final Loss
description: After what was likely Lionel Messi’s final World Cup appearance for Argentina, Antonela Roccuzzo shared a touching post about her husband. The post Lionel Messi’s Wife Sends Heartfelt Message After World Cup Final Loss appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
Although Argentina fell short of a second consecutive World Cup victory on Sunday, Lionel Messi had no reason to hang his head—a sentiment shared by his teammates, fans and most of all, his wife.

After what was likely Messi’s final World Cup appearance for La Albiceleste, Antonela Roccuzzo shared a two-photo post featuring a teary-eyed image of her husband alongside one of his teammates tossing him into the air. It was her way of suggesting that the good comes with the bad, while celebrating just how proud she was of him.

The photos were accompanied by a lengthy message directed to Messi.

“You will always be the best, @leomessi,” she beegan.

“Not just because of your talent, but because you have never stopped being yourself. Because no matter what happens, you never give up; you always fight to the very end and give it your all until the final second,” the caption read in Spanish (h/t NYPost).

“That strength, that mindset, and the way you pick yourself up time and time again are what make you unique. 🤍 Thank you for showing us every day that true success is built through hard work, sacrifice, and perseverance—without ever losing sight of who you really are.”

Antonela went on to praise the always-humble Messi for being a model citizen and a role model to their three children and millions of young fans across the world.

“You are the best example for our children and an inspiration to millions of people,” she continued “I admire you more than words can express, and I feel immensely proud to walk through life by your side. I love you so much. 🤍”

While Messi has yet to officially announce his retirement from international football, his social media post following the loss hinted at just that.

“The pain is immense, and it will take time for this wound to heal,” he wrote on IG.

Much like his wife, Messi cherished the good times leading into the World Cup final, which saw Argentina lose 1-0 to Spain in extra time.

“But I also hold on to all the good things. Today it is difficult to appreciate what we accomplished, but this group reached two consecutive World Cup finals,” he wrote, beaming with pride about his country’s accomplishments.

Messi went on to thank his teammates and fans for the wonderful journey

“Thank you from the bottom of my heart for every greeting and every message,” he added. “Once again, we managed to unite as a country and stand together, sharing the immense pride of being Argentine.”

title: Barcelona midfielder Gavi shares heartfelt message after World Cup glory: ‘I owe a great deal to everyone at La Masia’
description: Spain’s historic FIFA World Cup triumph continues to produce unforgettable moments, and Barcelona midfielder Gavi has now shared one of the most emotional stories to emerge from the celebrations. J...
fullText:
Spain’s historic FIFA World Cup triumph continues to produce unforgettable moments, and Barcelona midfielder Gavi has now shared one of the most emotional stories to emerge from the celebrations.

Just hours after helping Spain defeat Argentina 1-0 in the 2026 FIFA World Cup final, the 21-year-old reflected on his remarkable journey from a young footballer in Los Palacios to a world champion.

The midfielder shared a heartfelt social media post, where he thanked the people who influenced his career while looking back on the sacrifices that helped him reach football’s biggest stage.

Following Spain’s victory over Argentina, Gavi shared two photographs that perfectly captured his football journey.

One showed him playing as a youngster for La Liara Balompie in his hometown of Los Palacios, while the other featured him kissing the FIFA World Cup trophy after Spain were crowned world champions.

Accompanying the images was an emotional message that quickly resonated with supporters across the football world.

“May no one wake me from this dream – the dream of a boy from Los Palacios who dreamed of winning the World Cup with his country…”

The Barcelona midfielder also reflected on the setbacks he overcame before reaching the pinnacle of international football.

After battling injuries during his young career, Gavi admitted that every difficult moment had been worthwhile as he celebrated becoming a Spain World Cup winner at just 21 years of age.

Gavi made sure to acknowledge everyone who helped him along the way, explaining the role his family, close friends and former clubs played in his development.

Gavi has thanked everyone who influenced his career. (Photo by David Ramos/Getty Images)

The Spanish international thanked Real Betis, where his football journey began, before reserving special praise for FC Barcelona and La Masia, the academy that helped transform him into one of Europe’s brightest midfielders.

“I owe a great deal to everyone at La Masia who welcomed me from day one, and to the coaches and staff at FC Barcelona, who helped me grow as a footballer and as a person, and enabled me to become a world champion,” he wrote.

The Barcelona academy graduate also dedicated part of his message to his grandmother, thanking her for “that extra strength you gave me over the last few months.”

Although Gavi was not a regular starter during the tournament, he still contributed whenever called upon by Spain manager Luis de la Fuente.

The midfielder played 76 minutes across the competition, including a start against Cape Verde and a late appearance in the knockout victory over Austria.

title: Good news as Liverpool beat Bayern Munich to sign "new Kaka"
description: Liverpool has become a top destination for emerging young talents from all over the world. Rio Ngumoha is showing exactly what kind of pathway exists for the best young British prospects - set for a k...
fullText:
Liverpool has become a top destination for emerging young talents from all over the world.

Rio Ngumoha is showing exactly what kind of pathway exists for the best young British prospects - set for a ket role under Andoni Iraola next season aged only 17.

As well as securing big-money deals for Jeremy Jacquet and Giovanni Leoni the future of the backline has also been safeguarded by under-the-radar signings like Mor Talla Ndiaye and Ifeanyi Ndukwe.

The process is not limited to defenders only with Liverpool recruiters hard at work scouring the globe for the best young talent.

Of course overseas players are not permitted to officially sign for Liverpool until the age of 18 no matter what age they are identified.

In the case of Samuel Martinez for example he will be joining the club once he turns 18 next year. The Colombian playmaker, 17, will make the move from Atletico Nacional in his homeland next summer - for an initial fee of around £750k.

Martinez has been impressive at underage levels for his country - leading to Liverpool and other European clubs coming forward with their interest.

He jetted into England over the weekend to complete a medical and is expected to play a key first-team role for his original club before embarking on a Premier League adventure in 2027.

The news has been reported by TZ in the German media - where Bayern Munich were also thought to be in contention for Martinez’s signature.

The Bavarians have got the ideal mentor for the young schemer in Luis Diaz - who moved from Liverpool to FC Hollywood in 2025.

But Bayern are now regretting missing out on Martinez - whose talents have been talked up in the TZ report. Indeed it goes as far as describing Martinez as “the new Kaka” owing to his particular footballing characteristics.

Liverpool therefore look like they have signed an absolute gem - one of the best-rated young players on the South American continent. The 2024/25 Premier League champions have stolen a march on several top European rivals in order to land a deal.

Let’s hope he can go on to deliver - first for the youth and underage sides before making the step-up to the first team.
